responseSet.title: Response to Commentaries
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Commentaries

Joshua Levy

Joshua Levy

Joshua Levy  

I would like to express my heartfelt gratitude both to Bonnie Litowitz for accepting my case presentation for publication in JAPA and to the five colleagues who have provided their deeply engaged and much appreciated commentaries. In many respects this process of presentation and forthright collegial discussion is in keeping with the process that was engaged within the two workshops (held in 2011 and 2015) in which the case material was initially presented.

Freud and other analysts have elucidated the intimate relationship between patients’ dreams and underlying traumatic experiences. However, the process of analyzing dreams and working through those experiences in various phases of the analysis has rarely been addressed in the analytic literature.

The purpose of this presentation has been to highlight the working through of the patient's traumatic experiences by understanding and interpreting dreams in successive phases of the analysis and to do this in concert with a clear appreciation of the evolving transferences and countertransferences.

Briefly, the model of dream analysis that informed this analytic process involved an integration of both Freud's early model centering on the dreamwork, which he considered “the essence of dreaming” (Freud 1900, p. 650), and his later model of traumatic dreams as lacking dreamwork (Freud 1920; for further discussion of these two models, see Levy 2009, 2011). Relying on this integrated model and working with the patient's dreams allowed me to try to gauge Anne's response to the analytic interventions—whether they were helpful and advanced the analytic process or whether they were unhelpful and blocked the analytic process.

In attending to Anne's dreams I reflected on the multiple meanings of the manifest dream, seeing each dream as both revealing and disguising the patient's traumatic experiences. This reflection also helped me learn from the patient about the various influences, until then unrecognized, I had on her experience of the analytic process.

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin

Sonya Martin  

The complex interplay between intimacy and fear has informed psychoanalysis since Freud coined the term Oedipus complex in his own self-analysis. Approximately two decades later, in 1909, in “Analysis of a Phobia in a Five-Year-Old Boy,” he expounded on the castration anxiety of “Little Hans,” whose desire for intimacy with his mother led to a displaced fear of retaliation by father-like horses.

responseSet.title: An Exchange with Thomas Nagel: The Mind-Body Problem and Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Exchange with Thomas Nagel: The Mind-Body Problem and Psychoanalysis

Anne Erreich: The history of psychoanalytic theorizing has from the start been entangled with the mind-body problem. Freud's famous patients all had somatic complaints that led Freud to revolutionary discoveries about the mind. Freud himself attempted to bridge the mind/body problem with his “Project for a Scientific Psychology,” an effort he abandoned given the primitive level of neuroscience in his day. In abandoning neuroscience, Freud opted instead for a meaning-based theory in which he became a cryptographer struggling to decipher the meaning of his patients’ somatic and behavioral symptoms.

In recent years, the advent of new neuroscience technology, such as fMRI, has led some to attempt to revive Freud's dream of accounting for mind via brain.

The recent prevailing trend in psychiatry has been to reduce human subjective experience to objective characteristics of the brain as a physical organism. On this view, the subjective experience of depression would be understood solely as a chemical imbalance, and treated by an adjustment to brain chemistry. This stands in contrast to the traditional psychoanalytic view, which understands psychic phenomena such as depression as irreducibly tied to internal symbolic content, that is, meaning, in the form of images, thoughts, words, and behavior, to be decoded by the analyst and the patient.

Podcast audio version available at apa.sagepub.com. In addition to Thomas Nagel, participants include Anne Erreich, Training and Supervision Analyst, Institute for Psychoanalysis affiliated with NYU School of Medicine; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Child Study Center, NYU Langone Medical Center. Richard J. Kessler, Co-Chair, COPE Study Group on Psychoanalysis and Neuroscience, American Psychoanalytic Association; North American liaison, Neuropsychoanalyis Association. Barry Rand, faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Education affiliated with NYU School of Medicine. Jerome Wakefield, University Professor, Professor of Social Work, New York University; Professor of the Conceptual Foundations of Psychiatry, Department of Psychiatry, NYU School of Medicine.

responseSet.title: Commentary on Kernberg and Michels
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Kernberg and Michels

Steven Levy

Steven Levy

Tufts House
2004 Ridgewood Drive, NE
Atlanta, GA 30322
E-mail: <slevy@emory.edu>

Steven Levy  

Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels have provided a rich, nuanced, and fair-minded overview of a familiar perspective on what currently ails North American psychoanalytic education and its professional surround. They offer their take on steps needed to improve matters professionally, educationally, and organizationally. By way of full disclosure as a commentator on their contribution, I should note that, like both authors, I have spent my entire career as a full-time faculty member at a major American research university, which contains within its medical school psychiatry department the psychoanalytic institute to which I belong and in which I have held a variety of positions. Further, both Kernberg and Michels have had personal generative impact on my career development and have my gratitude, respect, and affection. We know each other well enough for them to easily recognize a gratuitous disclaimer for what is to follow. To be sure, I heartily agree with their view regarding the outdated usefulness of the current training analyst system and its often pernicious effects on institute life, creating an unfair hierarchy and undermining attention to improving educational methodology and developing research interests. I concur that developing a thoughtful, comprehensive, and transparent methodology for certifying psychoanalysts at a national level is possible and important in maintaining our professional standing, both among ourselves and with others in health care and in neighboring academic and professional disciplines.

That said, I would like to draw some distinctions, underline certain biases, many of which I have often shared, and offer for further consideration some tentative ideas that differ in emphasis and perspective from what Kernberg and Michels propose.

Bernard Holland Professor of Psychiatry, Emory University School of Medicine; former Editor, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

511

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Much Needs to Change in Analysis? How Do We Get There?

Beth Steinberg

Beth Steinberg

3321 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
E-mail: besteinberg@comcast.net

Beth Steinberg  

The symptom of this shadow zone is precisely the incapacity for an interchange with the ‘non-self’ which is unconsciously feared as dangerous and too disturbing. … In order not to succumb to the danger of petrification we would do well to heed the wise words … that our training never ends, and that, among other wise counsels, Freud left us the idea that from time to time we need to return to the analytic couch and be helped to regain the closeness with our unconscious.

—Stefano Bolognini (2011, p. 11)

To a packed house late Saturday afternoon at the Winter 2015 APsaA meetings, this ambitious panel was presented with the questions “How much needs to change in analysis? How do we get there?” Stanley Coen, as chair, introduced the panel, after which Peter Goldberg presented a clinical case; panelists Rosemary Balsam, Judith Yanof, and Aisha Abbasi and reporter Beth Steinberg then commented on the case and the issues it raised. As part of his presentation, Goldberg was asked to articulate his thoughts and feelings in response to the patient and his awareness of his reasons for each intervention during the session to allow both panel and audience to experience his way of thinking and working with his patient moment to moment.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 17, 2015. Panelists: Stanley J. Coen (chair), Peter Goldberg, Aisha Abbasi, Rosemary Balsam, Judith Yanof.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Levy and Finnegan

Henry Friedman

Henry Friedman

6 Garden Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: friedmanhj@aol.com

Henry Friedman   Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

It is both a privilege and a challenge to discuss an analytic case presented in such detail. I particularly welcome the opportunity to comment on the treating author's reliance on the analysis of dream material as crucial both to his understanding of the patient and to her progress at various stages of the analysis. In my comments I want to avoid any extrapolation from whether I agree or not with the author's reliance on dreams as a way of anchoring himself to his vision of what psychoanalysis required of this dyad, in this particular case, to a measure of my view of the importance of dreams in psychoanalyses more generally. I emphasize this distinction because I believe that the analyst in Anne's case was consistently distracted by his interest in analyzing her dreams, thereby failing to consider important aspects of her character as it interacted and clashed with his own. So while I attempt to elucidate my impressions of this analysis and the importance accorded the patient's many dreams, I need to clarify that my questioning of dream interpretation in this particular analysis indicates only my belief that close attention to dreams may distract both analyst and patient from attending to important dynamics in the treatment.

An overview of my perspective on this particular analytic dyad is important in understanding my doubts about the emphasis on dreams in this case. We have to begin with what I see as a considerable gap between the two participants in this analysis. The analyst is clearly a believer in dreams as the “royal road to the unconscious.” He is also a believer in the essential nature of free association and the need to associate to all “dream elements” and for the patient to always strive to say what is going through her mind.

Outcome Trajectories and Mediation in Psychotherapeutic Treatments of Major Depression

Günther Klug , Johannes Zimmermann and Dorothea Huber

Dorothea Huber

Sanatoriumsplatz 2
81545 München
GERMANY
E-mail: d.huber@lrz.tum.de

Dorothea Huber  

process-outcome, depression, mediator, introject, alliance

Trajectories and mediators of change were investigated in a process-outcome study. Patients were allocated at random to psychoanalytic therapy (PA) or psychodynamic therapy (PD), and later to cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT). Measurement points were at pre-treatment, during ongoing treatment, at post-treatment, and during a three-year follow-up. Outcome trajectories were assessed using the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI; Hautzinger et al. 1994), the Symptom Checklist 90 Revised Version (SCL-90-R; Franke 1995), and the Inventory of Interpersonal Problems (IIP; Horowitz, Strauss, and Kordy 2000). Therapeutic alliance and introject were tested as mediators, assessed using the Helping Alliance Questionnaire (HAQ; Bassler, Potratz, and Krauthauser 1995) and INTREX, introject surface (Tress 1993). Multilevel modeling was applied to estimate outcome trajectories and to test for mediation. Symptoms decreased in early and ongoing treatment in all treatment groups. After the end of treatment, depressive and general psychiatric symptoms continued to decrease in significantly greater degree in the PA group than in the PD and CBT cohorts. During early treatment, interpersonal problems decreased significantly more in those allocated to PD than in the PA and CBT groups. During ongoing treatment, improvement in interpersonal problems was significantly higher in the PA group than in the others and, compared to CBT, continued to increase significantly after termination. Mediational analyses revealed that neither introject affiliation nor therapeutic alliance mediated differential treatment effects.

The privilege of reading a memoir is the privilege of being close to the heart of the story of another person's life. In a psychoanalytic treatment, within the privacy and intimacy afforded by the psychoanalytic space created with our patient, we are similarly privileged to be close to the heart of a human story as, with our participation, the story unfolds and reaches toward deeper understanding and partial resolution. Either experience can offer us internal resonances, the chance to be moved and to learn through proximity to the story of another person's life. It is this kind of privilege that Madelon Sprengnether offers us in her new memoir, Great River Road.

Sprengnether's memoir is a literary narrative that depicts her efforts to build an integrated life narrative. It is a quest made particularly challenging by the devastating childhood experience of the accidental drowning of her beloved father.

Nancy Kulish   Training and Supervising Analyst and Past President, Michigan Psychoanalytic Institute; Adjunct Professor, Department of Psychiatry, School of Medicine at Wayne State Unicersity; Adjunct Professor of Psychology, University of Detroit/Mercy.

This book, dedicated to the memory of Helen Meyers, is an outgrowth of a conference sponsored by the Committee on Women and Psychoanalysis of the International Psychoanalytical Association, held in New York in 2014. The contributors—psychoanalysts, psychotherapists, poets, and academics, many of whom presented at the conference—examine mythic narratives about powerful female heroines and attempt to integrate these stories with contemporary models of female ambition, power, identity, and sexuality.

It is difficult, given this wide-ranging set of essays, to draw together common themes and conclusions. The editors suggest that the struggles of mythic heroines can be related to issues facing modern women and discuss implications of the myths for psychoanalytic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. They assert that exploring these tales of mighty women helped them tease out latent content in the narratives of their patients. For example, in her chapter on a very early myth from Sumer, written down two thousand years before the Old Testament was begun, Arlene Kramer Richards describes a scene in which the goddess Ianna looks at her vulva and declares herself Queen of Heaven. Richards links this scene to modern psychoanalytic understandings of female development involving primary femininity, or pride in the female genitals, rather than penis envy.

Some of the contributors use these mythical women quite directly as models to inspire their students or clients. For example, Merle Molofsky describes how contemporary women feel empowered by participating in a meditative and intense drumming called Taiko, an ancient Japanese tradition associated with the female deity of dawn and revelry. Similarly Ronnie Ancona finds that the figure of Medea inspires strong emotional responses and intellectual discussion among female students in a class at New York's Hunter College.

These attempts to apply myths directly to the lives of modern women do not do justice to their potential psychoanalytic meaning. To my mind, the power of a mythic figure to inspire insight or change comes naturally from within and not from without, as products of the culture and/or individual imagination.

An Appreciation of the Many Contributions of Leon Hoffman

Rosemary Balsam and Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber  

As coeditors of JAPA Review of Books since 2004, we regularly find ourselves filled with gratitude and admiration for the work of all our reviewers, for their avid reading, their appetitive attitude to all things psychoanalytic, and their dedication to writing and to sharing their opinions, enthusiasms, and critiques with our readership. They cheerfully (for the most part) put up with our input, our stylistic idiosyncratic quirks, our questions, comments, and back-and-forth, and finally our need for finishing details on their manuscripts. We greatly enjoy working with the texts of our reviewers, both those who have been invited and those who have volunteered.

Leon Hoffman has been an outstanding contributor to our section, as you can see from the list below, in the quantity and quality of his many reviews over the years from before we assumed our editorial positions. We personally rely on him to keep us updated on the interface between psychiatry and psychoanalysis, including empirical studies, work with both children and adults, and efforts in teaching, learning, and stretching our field in this interdisciplinary area. His reviews invariably draw compliments from our readers for how lucidly and succinctly he can frame whatever theoretical or clinical discussion is the subject of inquiry. Many have stressed how extremely helpful they find his work and how it has provided a guide to the important books he chooses to attend to. We believe it is time to offer Leon a special thank you from us, and on behalf of many of our readers.

Leon Hoffman is a psychiatrist and child and adolescent psychiatrist, a training and supervising analyst in adult, child, and adolescent analysis at the New York Psychoanalytic Institute, and director of the Pacella Parent Child Center and co-director of the Pacella Research Center at the New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute. He is on the faculty of the Icahn Medical School at Mount Sinai Hospital, serves as chief psychiatrist at the West End Day School in New York, and co-chairs APsaA's Committee on Research Education (CORE).

Lynne Zeavin  

This very interesting clinical material gives us a welcome opportunity to reflect on the meaning of dreams in analysis and the ways we address and think of them—particularly when the patient is silent about them or is otherwise reluctant to engage directly with the analyst. The paper is offered as a commentary on the possibilities of dreams as a means of working through trauma. For me, though, what seems in play with these dreams is not so much trauma and its resolution, but rather how the dreams depict the psychoanalytic process itself. I think of the dreams as a reflection and commentary on the ongoing psychoanalytic process, representing the patient's internal world as it plays out in the workings of the transference and countertransference. I think more of how the dream functions than of what it might “say.”

The analyst describes his sensitivity to his patient's sense of fragility, a sensitivity that from the outset, I think, exerts a particular grip on the analysis. With that in mind I was particularly interested in the state of mind the dreams arouse in the analyst. For example, the analyst's response might take the form of an overriding and inhibiting concern about trying to get it just right with his patient. The patient's dreams, then, would not only depict her anxieties and fantasies, but would also transmit those anxieties to the analyst. The analyst may unwittingly enact these anxieties by taking an overly careful approach to the dream material.

I don't accord dreams a privileged place in the analytic work. Once a dream is told, I think immediately of how both the dream and its telling are used by the patient. Does she allow the analyst room to think about the dream? Do interpretations excite her or shut her down? Does the analyst feel he has to fit in with the patient's requirements? I don't think we should treat dreams as unique modes of expression.

Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

85

Freud's Enduring Legacy

Freud and the Spoken Word: Speech as a Key to the Unconscious. By Ana-Maria Rizzuto. New York: Routledge, 2015, xvi + 196 pp., $190.00 hardcover, $52.95 paperback.

David Goldenberg

David Goldenberg

35 East 85th Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: <davidgoldenbergmd@gmail.com>

David Goldenberg   Clinical Assistant Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Assistant Attending Psychiatrist, New York-Presbyterian Hospital, Department of Psychiatry.

I have always been struck by the limited theorizing about the function of language in psychoanalysis. I am not talking about linguistics. J am talking about a theory of the living acts of speech in the analytic situation, which go beyond semantics to become psychic acts between two persons.

—ana-maria rizzuto (1992, pp. 126-127)

Ana-Maria Rizzuto is a prolific author, theoretician, clinician, and psychoanalytic historian. Having published papers on a wide range of subjects, she seems to have a special interest in speech, listening, and the construction of the mind through internal representations of relationships and affect, topics inspired by Freud's 1891 monograph On Aphasia: A Critical Study. Like many others, Rizzuto sees the origins of Freud's theories and clinical practice in this early work, as well as its influence on and persistence in the rest of his life's work. Her interest is substantial, as she has become “a devotee of the monograph,” publishing her “detailed analysis of several aspects of it in five papers” (p. 4). She further distinguishes herself with Freud and the Spoken Word: Speech as the Key to the Unconscious, in which she meticulously traces the influence of that monograph through his complete oeuvre. She has undertaken a vast scholarly study identifying the persistence, through the entirety of Freud's works, of his early ideas from On Aphasia, as well as seeking support for her belief in the unique and primary importance of “the spoken word.”

At the outset Rizzuto states, “I disagree with Freud's rejection of his early masterpiece.

In the contemporary mental health care scene, too many psychiatric leaders join the insurance industry in debunking psychodynamic approaches to treatment, despite many studies of their effectiveness (Wampold 2001: Hoffman 2009, 2015a,b; Levy et al. 2014). The two books under review here are a welcome antidote to these naysayers (see Coyne 2012). The volume by Luyten, Mayes, Fonagy, Target, and the late Sidney Blatt addresses the empirical foundations of psychodynamic approaches; the volume by MacKinnon, Michels, and Buckley, the third edition of a volume first published in 1971 and updated in 2006, stresses the value of the dialogue between patient and clinician. The chapters in the first volume are written by a range of authors, all of them notable contributors to the psychodynamic empirical literature; the second includes chapters by three contributors in addition to the principal authors. The Handbook stresses empirical approaches to theoretical and clinical issues, while the Psychiatric Interview, much more practically oriented, will be especially helpful to beginners learning to approach patients.

Both volumes are quite long and, like most textbooks, are not meant to be read in a few sittings. Rather, they are best utilized as introductory and source material when learning about (or teaching) particular topics.

Arnold Richards   Former Editor, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Joseph Berke is a psychoanalytic psychotherapist who works with families. He trained at Columbia University and Albert Einstein College of Medicine in New York, but has lived in London since 1965. He went there to study with R. D. Laing and helped him establish the Kingsley Hall Community, a residential treatment facility for the emotionally disturbed and impaired. He is known for his treatment of Mary Barnes, diagnosed with schizophrenia, who was helped dramatically through treatment “regression” and became a “noted artist, author, and mystic” (Barnes and Berke 2002, p. 14). With her he wrote Mary Barnes: Two Accounts of a Journey through Madness (2002), later adapted as a stage play. There have been other accounts in the literature of the emergence of talented individuals from mental turmoil. A recent example is Elyn Saks, who wrote The Center Cannot Hold (2007).

Berke has written extensively on the treatment of serious emotional disorders. This book, as well as Sigmund Freud and the Lubavich Rebbe (Berke 2015), connects that area of his work with his other interests: Freud studies, Judaism, and Kabbalah. Here he tries to make the case that there is a profound connection between psychoanalysis and the Kabbalah, a connection he first presented in “Psychoanalysis and Kabbalah” in 1996. The Freud Studies section of Centers of Power: Convergence of Psychoanalysis and Kabbalah (Berke and Schneider 2008) details his “discovery” that Freud treated the future Lubavitcher Rebbe, the Rashab, in the first decade of the twentieth century. This fact has been well known in Lubavitcher circles for decades. The story is that the Rebbe, who was living with his followers in Riga, Latvia, became depressed. His group was harassed by the Latvian Government, which was encouraged by the Mitnagdim, the “Jewish enemies” of the Lubavitcher Chasidim. He was advised by members of his following to travel to Vienna and seek treatment from Freud.

responseSet.title: “Unavoidable Satisfactions”: The Analyst's Pleasure
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Unavoidable Satisfactions”: The Analyst's Pleasure

Michael Shulman

Michael Shulman

117 North First Street, Suite 113
Ann Arbor, Ml 48104
E-mail: drmichaelshulman@gmail.com

Michael Shulman  

pleasure, pain, desire, technique, psychoanalytic vocation, countertransference, boundary violation

Analysts have often described their work as depriving, painful, and hard to endure, while its pleasures have been the subject of little commentary. The real history and ongoing temptations of boundary violation long ago made the gratifications of psychoanalytic work a matter of anxiety. Analysts' pleasure in their work was problematized. Some of this problematizing is necessary because of real risk, but much of it is not only unnecessary but misleading and destructive. Psychoanalysts pursue achievement of a unique form of human intimacy, yet acquired habits of professional modesty and humility have encouraged the illusion that analyzing can occur without desire or ambition on the analyst's part. These habits have made it difficult for analysts to openly discuss what they get from the intimacy of analyzing that yields its pleasures. Our field demands that analysts deny that the work provides much more than pain (at least until the conclusion of an analysis), but psychoanalysis both misunderstands and misrepresents itself if we cannot speak of the distinctly broad range of pleasures available in analyzing.

Laplanche: From the Enigmatic Message of the other to the Unconscious Alterity within

The Temptation of Biology: Freud's Theories of Sexuality. By Jean Laplanche. Translated by Donald Nicholson-Smith. New York: The Unconscious in Translation, 2015, xii + 140 pp., $73.50 hardcover, $39.50 paperback.Between Seduction and Inspiration: Man. By Jean Laplanche. Translated and with an introduction by Jeffrey Mehlman. New York: The Unconscious in Translation, 2015, xxii + 304 pp., $68.50 hardcover, $48.50 paperback.

Deborah Browning

Deborah Browning

29 East 9th Street, #4
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: deborah.browning@nyu.edu

Deborah Browning   Adjunct Associate Professor, Department of Psychology, New York University Graduate School of Arts and Science (retired); member, American Psychoanalytic Association.

These two books, The Temptation of Biology: Freud's Theories of Sexuality (hereafter, Temptation) and Between Seduction and Inspiration: Man (hereafter, Seduction), by the French psychoanalyst Jean Laplanche are complete English translations, compiled by Laplanche himself, of his major writings and lectures from 1992 through 1999 and published here in 2015 by the American press The Unconscious in Translation. Although Laplanche's name is well known, linked with J.-B. Pontalis as co-author of The Language of Psycho-Analysis (1967; hereafter, Language), as well as from his Life and Death in Psychoanalysis (1970) and New Foundations for Psychoanalysis (1987), it is important to keep in mind that until his death in 2012 Laplanche was also in charge of the complete translation of the works of Freud from German into French, translating many of the papers himself. This process provided him a particularly intimate knowledge of certain aspects of Freud's writing, not easily available to the exclusively anglophone reader of Strachey's Standard Edition. Importantly, Laplanche's thinking and writing stem also from his clinical psychoanalytic work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Greed: Developmental, Cultural, and Clinical Realms. Edited by Salman Akhtar. London: Karnac Books, 2015, xviii + 245 pp., $43.95 paperback.

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

11 Mountain Laurel Lane
Rhinebeck, NY 12572
E-mail: mcavell@earthlink.net

Marcia Cavell   Associate Professor of Philosophy Emerita, Purchase College, State University of New York; CORST graduate, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

With the exception of Melanie Klein, psychoanalysts have not paid greed the attention that it deserves given its importance in human experience, a neglect that Salman Akhtar seeks to redress in this splendid volume, richer by far than its small size would suggest.

Dante consigned the greedy to the seventh circle of hell. Though it has a more benign use, “greed” is a term of opprobrium; it is, I would guess, one of the first moral concepts we learn: “Don't be greedy,” and “One is enough!” our mothers said. Greed was the other side of the equally early injunction to clean our plates: “Think of the starving Armenians” (the nationality changed with the generation). Greed, starvation, and waste. Greed and limits. Greed and the oral—a “voracious” love of books, a “hunger” for fame, a “craving” for clothes, a “thirst” for knowledge, an “appetite” for cruelty, being “starved” for affection. Greed and matter: food, then, fast on its heels, money, which hedges against starvation, and which can symbolically substitute for loss.

“One is enough.” What is “enough”? Enough for what? Measured how? What determines the limit, or the proper limit, of the object of greed? A defining characteristic is that it knows no limits, it is insatiable. In the background here lurk the ideas of scarcity and need, scarcity and justice: “Don't take more than your fair share!” Possessed by greed, we delude ourselves that want is need.

In the case of literal hunger, the idea of a limit is defined by the vague idea of what is necessary for health. We note, incidentally, that too much food is as bad as too little. Often we do things that are bad for us unintentionally, or inadvertently, and sometimes because what we consciously do means something else unconsciously: food as an appeasement to anxiety, as a replacement for love, or as a compensation.

1069

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Unseen and the Unheard in Fiction and Memoir

Gurmeet Kanwal

Gurmeet Kanwal

275 Central Park West, Suite 1W
New York, NY 10024-3025
E-mail: gkanwalmd@gmail.com

Gurmeet Kanwal  

As a psychoanalyst one cannot help but ask, What is the difference between “memoir” and “fiction”? Between memory and fantasy? Evolutionists would suggest that memory has evolved in order to help us survive. However, nature, in its relentless pilgrimage toward some as yet unknown mecca of evolution, rarely follows a straight path, and has never been free of imperfections. Thankfully, it is not only the fittest that survive. Imperfections, in fact, are nature's artistic idiom. A modus vivendi between Catholic Destiny and Agnostic Chance. Perhaps it is these imperfections in the evolution of memory that make fantasy possible. Indeed, fiction may well be the accidental by-product of memoir!

The writers Chris Abani and Zadie Smith, invited speakers at APsaA's 2016 University Forum1 (with the psychoanalyst Stanley Coen as chair and the psychoanalyst/poet Alice Jones as discussant), held in New York on January 15, have produced a body of work that very naturally inhabits the stateless territory between psychology and art. Like the immigrant families they come from, their writings travel back and forth between continents, races, ethnicities, cultures, and all the conflicts and trauma that abound in these spaces of human experience. For this occasion, Abani presented an essay titled “Painting a Body of Loss and Love in the Proximity

1For an introduction to the University Forums, see Coen (2015). In a personal communication regarding the current series, Coen said, “Here the unseen and unheard is from the perspective of Western white people, including American psychoanalysts, whose eyes and ears need to be opened.” I find myself quite aware that, unlike the two previous reporters of these forums, I write as an immigrant neither “white” nor “Western.”

Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Supervising Psychoanalyst and faculty, William Alanson White Institute of Psychiatry, Psychoanalysis, and Psychology.

Submitted for publication March 27, 2016.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Clinical Case Presentation: Understanding and Interpreting Dreams While Working through Developmental Trauma

Joshua Levy and Paul Finnegan

Paul Finnegan

35 Castlefrank Road
Toronto, Ontario
CANADA, M4W 2Z5
E-mail: josh.levy@utoronto.ca

554 Spadina Avenue
Toronto, Ontario
CANADA, M5S 2J9
E-mail: pfinnegan@sympatico.ca

Paul Finnegan  

understanding and interpreting dreams, developmental trauma, therapeutic alliance, crisis, working through, termination, dissociation, transference, countertransference

The purpose of this paper is to demonstrate the unique place of understanding and interpreting dreams in the psychoanalytic process while working through developmental trauma. This psychoanalytic process extended over six years and is presented in four phases: establishing the therapeutic alliance, a crisis, working through, and termination. Dreams from each of these four phases of the analysis are presented, and the collaborative work of understanding and interpreting these dreams is highlighted. Evidence is presented that from this analytic work there ensued an amelioration of the impact of developmental trauma and a furtherance of the development of internal psychic structure.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Levy and Finnegan

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro

E-mail: ninoferro3@gmail.com

Antonino Ferro  

I greatly appreciate this article and the treating analyst for the depth of his reflections, his approach to working through the countertransference, his delicacy toward the patient's needs and sensitivities, and his capacity to be in the here and now of her story and external reality.

I was also impressed by the sensitivity of the analyst's interpretations, reflecting his capacity to gauge the distance and depth tolerable to the patient. His willingness to doubt and question his own contribution to what happened in the session is also noted.

In short, this is an analyst I would gladly have had as my own.

I will now attempt to outline how my own approach would differ from his.

First, when a case is presented to me, I tend to consider what is recounted as a dream the analyst has dreamed to communicate as much as possible about the analytic process.

Further, I consider the people or functions that enter a session as characters of the session itself, fruit, if you will, of the interaction between two minds, the patient's and the analyst's, or, rather, of the analytic field created by the two minds.

Moreover, my approach is to deconstruct, deconcretize, and redream the patient's communications during the session.

For instance, I would consider the initial story as a description of how the patient's behavior could swing from childlike and angelic to more adult, rebellious, and aggressive. I would consider the rebellious and aggressive older brother as a character in the field who has long sought a means of expression: a split-off bound and gagged character underlying the patient's depression.

The trauma, in my view, is constituted by the fact that the patient lives and has lived in an oversaturated atmosphere of violence and unthinkable proto-emotions.

55

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mutuality, Recognition, and the Self: Psychoanalytic Reflections. By Christine C. Kieffer. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xxxiv + 203 pp., $39.95 paperback.

Stephen Atkins

Stephen Atkins

251 Edwards Street
New Haven, CT 06511
E-mail: sratkins@optonline.net

Stephen Atkins   Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis; Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine.

The self has always stirred controversy among psychoanalysts. How do we integrate descriptions of immediate, subjective experiences of self with descriptions of more abstract aspects of agency and motivation? In traditional ego psychology, for example, “the primary motivators are the drives, with the ego consisting of a set of functions used in managing the drives and mediating between the drives and the outside world. The self is conceptualized as a representation, or set of contents within the structure of the ego. In contrast, … self psychologists argue that the self is a structure with its own driving force or motivations that are primary and irreducible” (Auchincloss and Samberg 2012, p. 232). In Mutuality, Recognition, and the Self, Christine Kieffer presents a view of self development from the standpoint of self psychology integrated with both intersubjective and relational perspectives.

In a prologue she elaborates several features of this view. First, it is based on a two-person model of the mind, a paradigm change that understands clinical phenomena such as transference and projective identification as co-constructed by analyst and patient. For Kieffer, following Aron, psychic structure formation in analysis is based not only on the stable characteristics of analyst and patient taken separately (a one-person psychology) but also on properties that emerge from the interaction between them (a two-person psychology). “Mutual recognition develops as two people meet and engage with one another—within a context of bi-directional influence. However, mutual recognition, in which there is an awareness and acceptance of the other's autonomy, is … often a developmental achievement. Thus, therapeutic action is aimed at helping the patient to develop a cohesive state that permits a sense of one's subjectivity while recognising the subjectivity of the other” (p. xxii).

211

[[RunningHead]]

Varieties of Creativity: Theory, Procreation, Teaching Psychoanalysis

Creative Analysis: Art, Creativity and Clinical Process. By George Hagman. London: Routledge, 2015, xiv + 129 pages, $178.95 hardcover, $42.95 paperback.

Nancy Olson

Nancy Olson

47 Trumbull Street
New Haven, CT 06510-1004
E-mail: <nancy.olson@yale.edu>

Nancy Olson   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine; faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

“I sometimes think you could be more creative as a therapist.”1 I've never forgotten this comment from a gifted supervisor, made in the course of a positive yet otherwise unremarkable evaluation. It made me see how concerned I had been with “doing it right,” and how this might distract me from creative aspects of the work. I came to medicine from art history and was happy to think of psychiatry as creative. But as a new therapist, I was a dutiful pupil in the École des Beaux-Arts, bent on the mastery of established technique, with little sense as yet of innovation or personal style.

What is creative analysis? George Hagman's new book is a gallery tour of the subject, opening with a brief survey of what analysts have said about creative processes: Freud's comparison of creative writing to daydreams and the child's imaginary play, Kris's regression in the service of the ego, Klein's attempt to repair internal objects, and Winnicott's transitional space between the inner life and the object world. The book goes on to offer a description of the creative process that applies especially well to the analytic endeavor. This is then elaborated, with particular attention to the creativity of both participants.

Following Winnicott and influenced by self psychology, Hagman considers the work of art a type of transitional object, invested with fantasy and experienced as part of the self (p. 4). To begin, the artist makes a mark (in her chosen medium)

The Rupture of Serenity: External Intrusions and Psychoanalytic Technique. By Aisha Abbasi. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xviii + 166 pp., $39.95.

Megan Poe

Megan Poe

175 West 12th Street, #2N
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: meganpoemd@gmail.com

Megan Poe   Clinical Assistant Professor, Department of Child and Adolescent Psychiatry, NYU School of Medicine.

Aisha Abbasi is to be thanked for writing us into the scope of her gifts. The gift that stands out most is her courage, and the ability to let us into her real-time inner life as an analyst—not offering us those polished, oversupervised, too-smooth stones we have all held, but her in-real-time inner life, those rough-looking geodes cracked open. She takes us along as she becomes a better and better analyst. She shares the good and the bad, and joins us in curiously observing them. One has the experience of pulling up a chair at a table across from an extraordinary person who also happens to be an analyst, and listening to her unalloyed accounts of her work with patients. She does not try to control her readers or their perceptions. She treats each of us like a trusted colleague.

The Rupture of Serenity is focused on what Abbasi calls the “external intrusions” from within the analytic process. They are the “uninvited guests” at the psychoanalytic dinner table. She describes her own infertility treatments and pregnancy barging into the dyad; swapping envy with her patients; her vulnerability attending disability or illness. She describes incidents when patients learn something “real” about us—for her, the fact of her own transgender child. Even more intrusively, patients learned about how her family handled the situation. Abbasi ventures into habitats we would rather not look at, such as the waiting room, where we do not control how our patients interact with it or with other patients; or the i-world—how technology intrudes, as when she lent a patient her iPhone after his car broke down and he i-plundered it for videos of her family. She speaks of her own and her patients' reactions to learning that Osama Bin Laden was found hiding in Abbottabad, Pakistan, where she had spent many years as a child and adolescent. Abbasi lets us in.

To read this book is to listen responsively to how one's own mind works, as she zooms in and out of different analytic “distances,” touching different analytic depths of field, and pointing precisely as needed. She must be an outstanding teacher. Following are some virtues that resonated with me and my experience in treating patients.

The Americanization of Narcissism. By Elizabeth Lunbeck. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2014, vi + 367 pp., $35.00 hardcover.

Richard Munich

Richard Munich

185 Madison Avenue, Suite 15-10
New York, NY 10016
E-mail: rlmunich@gmail.com

Richard Munich   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

… the processes of the formation of character are more obscure and less accessible to analysis than neurotic ones.

—Freud (1913)

… if voters select narcissistic individuals as their leaders, they may end up with gifted stage performers who are persuasive and decisive. They may, however, also get a good deal more than they bargained for.

—Lilienfeld and Watts (2015)

This elegant and informative contribution describes the personal, psychological, psychoanalytic, and sociocultural origins and up-to-the-moment development of the concept of narcissism, especially in North America. Perhaps the most creative aspect of the work is the integration and synthesis of intensely conflicting points of view. The author's risk in taking on the issues and diversity of language among the various fields is managed with an extensive scholarship that devotes nearly a third of the book to endnotes. This research is supported by Lunbeck's evenness of tone and respect for her sources.

The book comprises two main sections: the first offers a description of the issues and an overall look at the work of Christopher Lasch, Heinz Kohut, and Otto Kernberg in the 1970s and 80s; the second examines more specific aspects of narcissism from Freud up to the present. Contributors to the controversies surrounding narcissism include social critics like David Riesman and and William Whyte, as well as analysts like Jones, Ferenczi, Riviere, Winnicott, and Erikson, all of whom are considered in some depth. Again, a central theme is Lunbeck's refreshing effort to reconcile opposing points of view rather than simply presenting the positive and negative aspects of the narcissistic character. This is done by including personality factors in the equation, as seen in letters and from material regarding the lives of the various authors, as well as in their formal, written work and therapeutic practice.

Road Trips on the Via Regia: Introduction to Levy and Finnegan

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

The interpretation of dreams is in fact the royal road to a knowledge of the unconscious.

—Freud (1910, p. 33)

Freud made his “royal road” remark in the third of his 1909 lectures at Clark University, where he introduced the new science of psychoanalysis to an American audience. He found the phrase so felicitous that he included it in the second edition of The Interpretation of Dreams (Freud 1900, p. 608). The idea of a journey—a road trip, as we might say today—was very much on Freud's mind when he was writing the Dream Book in 1899. In a letter to Fliess (August 6, 1899), Freud describes his use of the journey as an orienting metaphor (Lakoff and Johnson 1980) for leading the reader through this new terrain of psychoanalysis1:

The whole thing is planned on the model of an imaginary walk. First comes the dark wood of the authorities (who cannot see the trees), where there is no clear view and it is easy to go astray. Then there is a cavernous defile through which I lead my readers … and then, all at once, the high ground and the open prospect and the question: “Which way do you want to go?” [1900, p. 122 n].

We followed Freud on that trip to discover that a dream expresses a repressed wish (the latent dream thoughts) in another form (manifest dream images)

1In this letter one can clearly hear echoes of the opening lines of Dante's Divine Comedy, in which the poet's guide on his journey to the underworld is Virgil, whose Aeneid in turn provides Freud his epigraph for the The Interpretation of Dreams.9

Angela Cappiello   Faculty, Western New England Psychoanalytic Society; Medical Staff and Supervisor, Institute of Living, Hartford.

The phenomenon of screen memories seems to have been forgotten in contemporary psychoanalytic theory, and to have lost its relevance in current clinical practice.

In this book the editors, Gail S. Reed and Howard B. Levine, reexamine Freud's original paper on screen memories (1899) through the lens of contemporary psychoanalysis. They present a rounded view of recent analytic thinking on screen memories by collecting essays from eight prestigious psychoanalysts, diverse in their theoretical and geographical backgrounds, and challenge the reader with discussions on the current therapeutic applications of screen memories.

In their introduction Reed and Levine write that “whether one is referring to the concept, the paper by Freud, or the general clinical phenomenon, screen memories are full of rich and complex psychoanalytic ideas.

Commentary on Kernberg and Michels

Peter Fonagy and Elizabeth Allison

Elizabeth Allison

Gower Street
London WC1E 6BT
UNITED KINGDOM
E-mail: <p.fonagy@ucl.ac.uke.allison@ucl.ac.uk>

Elizabeth Allison  

Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels, along with most readers of this journal, are intellectually and emotionally committed to psychoanalysis as a science, as a clinical practice, and as a moral-ethical framework. Both men are among our most distinguished psychoanalytic educators, and when they propose a new model for psychoanalytic education, anyone concerned with the future of psychoanalysis should listen and take note.1

Their paper links the vulnerability of the educational system to the troubles that beset psychoanalysis. They point to the field's increasing distance from the nucleus of modern culture and the central position it occupied for much of the last century. The standards that most psychoanalytic institutes use to assess appropriate knowledge, skill, and competence for qualification as either analyst or training analyst are loosely specified, subjectively assessed, and prone to perverse incentives, and therefore do not adequately protect individuals or groups from potentially iniquitous processes. The authors' basic claim, that mechanisms for quality control in psychoanalysis are lacking, finds echoes in many recent appraisals of its systems (Coopersmith 2010; Schneider et al. 2014; Szecsödy 2008; Wallerstein 2007; Zimmer 2003).

Kernberg and Michels go on to link their pedagogical challenges to the far deeper problem of the relationship of psychoanalytic institutions

1Peter Fonagy and Elizabeth Allison both trained with the British Psychoanalytic Society at the Institute of Psychoanalysis in London, qualifying in 1985 and 2010 respectively. They share a concern with the future of the discipline and its relationship to scientific inquiry and higher education.Peter Fonagy, Freud Memorial Professor and Head of the Research Department of Clinical, Educational and Health Psychology, University College London; Chief Executive, the Anna Freud Centre. Elizabeth Allison, Director of the Psychoanalysis Unit, Research Department of Clinical, Educational and Health Psychology, University College London.

Lynne Layton  

The institute in which I trained, and now teach and supervise, the Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis, is a member neither of the American Psychoanalytic Association nor of the International Psychoanalytical Association. A major reason for this is that we chose not to have a training analyst system. Candidates can choose their analysts and supervisors from among all who are certified as graduate analysts, from any institute. Upon graduation, all are deemed qualified to supervise and teach. A tenet of the institute is that candidates are adult learners, and all graduates are assumed to be competent once they have completed the required coursework, supervisions, and a final project. Our founding curriculum included a course in gender and a course in culture, both of which remain in the curriculum. I taught my first courses at the institute in the very first years of its establishment, long before I was an analyst. When I began teaching in the early nineties, I had finished training as a psychoanalytically oriented clinical psychologist, but my area of expertise, psychoanalytic feminist gender theory, was developed not in my training as a clinician but during my previous training as a Ph.D. in comparative literature. While teaching, serving on committees, and eventually training at my institute, I also taught courses in psychoanalysis and gender and in psychoanalysis and culture in Harvard's women's studies and social studies departments from 1989 to 2005. When I first began teaching gender at my institute, several papers that we taught came from the academic world, but by around 2005, when I began teaching psychoanalysis and culture there, we were able to find enough papers written by

Commentary on Kernberg and Michels

Nancy McWilliams

Nancy McWilliams

9 Mine Street
Flemington, NJ 08822-1515
E-mail: <nancymcw@aol.com>

Nancy McWilliams  

Having been involved all my professional life in psychoanalytic education, I am delighted to have been asked to comment on this paper by Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels on a matter close to my heart.

My own experience as a teacher differs somewhat from theirs. It involves less immersion in institute-based education and perhaps more work with professionals new to psychoanalysis, with psychoanalytically oriented therapists who are not fully trained analysts, and with analysts trained outside the American Psychoanalytic Association. My own analytic education was entirely outside APsaA, and so I have only a partial understanding of the complexities of its controversies about certification, the training analyst system, and similar issues.

Thus, although I am an insider to efforts to improve analytic education, I am an outsider to the battles in APsaA and the IPA, of which both Kernberg and Michels are veterans. Perhaps because of my outsider status, despite finding myself in accord with the spirit of their critique of analytic training, I have some questions about the means for improvement that they suggest, both jointly and separately. I am more radical than either author on questions of training, probably because I have not been part of the systems they are endeavoring to change.

The pleas in their paper for theoretical diversity, egalitarianism, and transparency mirror the sensibility that characterized my psychoanalytic training in the early 1970s at the National Psychological Association for Psychoanalysis. NPAP fostered a notably democratic learning culture in which a range of analytic ideas were taught.

Visiting Full Professor, Graduate School of Applied and Professional Psychology, Rutgers University.

517

Commentary on Kernberg and Michels

Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

3667 Sacramento Street
San Francisco, CA 94118
E-mail: <stephen.seligman@ucsf.edu>

Stephen Seligman  

It is a privilege to comment on Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels's “Thoughts on the Present and Future of Psychoanalytic Education.” Their paper reflects their insight and experience regarding all aspects of psychoanalytic institutional, intellectual, and clinical life, in the context of many decades of leadership in psychiatry and medicine. All of this affords them a rare platform from which to diagnose and offer solutions for problems on the psychoanalytic scene. Indeed, they offer a wide-ranging and outspoken analysis of the current status of our field. Their broad and deep familiarity with these matters has already been evidenced by their extensive contributions to the literature.

This paper is timely, as the American Psychoanalytic Association is reforming the training analyst system and reevaluating the relationship between local institutes and the national, as these authors note. In addition, there is indeed much concern among analysts, in the U.S. and elsewhere, about the marginalization of analysis in the mental health care market and in academic departments of psychiatry, psychology, and social work, as well as in the broader culture. Although the two authors have substantial disagreements about the sources of the current difficulties (which they delineate openly), Kernberg and Michels offer a pungent analysis of these problems. They are united and typically forceful in their call for clearer, more objective methods to evaluate psychoanalytic competence in education and promotion. Such methods, they predict, would lead to changes in both organizational governance and curriculum, including the waning of the TA system, a prospect they deem desirable.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California, San Francisco; Joint Editor-in-Chief, Psychoanalytic Dialogues; Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California.

525

Confidentiality and Its Discontents: Dilemmas of Privacy in Psychotherapy. By Paul W. Mosher and Jeffrey Berman. New York: Fordham University Press, 2015, xiv + 343 pp., $125.00 hardcover, $37.00 paperback.

Elyn Saks

Elyn Saks

699 Exposition Boulevard
Los Angeles, CA 90089-0071
E-mail: esaks@law.usc.edu

Elyn Saks   Orrin B. Evans Professor of Law, Psychology, and Psychiatry and the Behavioral Sciences, University of Southern California Gould School of Law; faculty and Psychoanalytic Associate, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles.

Paul Mosher and Jeffrey Berman's Confidentiality and Its Discontents: Dilemmas of Privacy in Psychotherapy is a wonderful account of these dilemmas. The title is telling: playing on Freud's “Discontents” title, it

1081

highlights that therapists are faced with unwelcome and painful demands that may pull against the patient's therapeutic interests.

Mosher and Berman rightly point out how vital confidentiality would seem to be to psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic therapy. Patients will be loath to follow the “fundamental rule” to “say everything that comes to mind” unless they are confident that what they say will never leave the room. A condition of full disclosure is confidentiality. If this cannot be promised—or if the patient is actually warned of the limits of confidentiality—he or she will not be fully forthcoming.

A couple of things can be said here. First, it is just an article of faith that confidentiality is necessary. Logically it would seem to be. But often things that seem likely theoretically don't pan out that way empirically. Consider that it is a reality that confidentiality is not absolute, and yet treatments, good treatments, happen anyway. The idea that confidentiality is not absolute may not affect many people, though there is good evidence, seen in some of Mosher and Berman's case studies, that actual breaches of confidentiality do.

Does lack of confidentiality introduce complications? Maybe. Perhaps it would be well to study this empirically: one group of patients, say, could be told about the limits of confidentiality, while another group would not be. Do the former free associate about this? Does concern manifest in unconscious phenomena? Do patients given such warnings disclose less potentially damning information and, for example, stay away from revealing angry thoughts? Of course studying the effects when warnings to potential victims are given would be useful.

The second thing is that the psychodynamic manifestations of learning that confidentiality is not absolute and may be lost bear looking into. It would have been useful had Mosher and Berman tried to tease out more of this. For instance, do patients unconsciously feel betrayed by the threat of loss of confidentiality? Do they lose trust in the analyst and the analytic process? Do they fear abandonment? Do they feel unconscious rage at what the “parents” are doing to them? Speculating about this, and indeed—again—studying it could be very useful.

Robert Stolorow

Robert Stolorow  

In an article in JAPA 64/3, Lawrence Friedman addresses a question he takes as his title: “Is There a Usable Heidegger for Psychoanalysts?” I am happy to report that this question has already been answered in my own work with a resounding “Yes!” Let me approach the question by first giving some background.

During the early and mid-1970s, George Atwood and I conducted a series of psychobiographical studies of the work and lives of four psychoanalytic theorists—Freud, Jung, Reich, and Rank—in each case showing how the experiential world of the theorist contributed to the shaping of his theory. In the final chapter of Faces in a Cloud (Stolorow and Atwood 1979)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Intergenerational Transmission of Suicide: Moral Injury and the Mysterious Object in the Work of Walker Percy

Jane Tillman

Jane Tillman

25 Main Street
Stockbridge, MA 01262
E-mail: <jane.tillman@austenriggs.net>

Jane Tillman  

object relations, identification, suicide, moral injury, intergenerational transmission of trauma

The intrapsychic mechanisms for the intergenerational transmission of suicide are not adequately theorized, though it is well known that a family history of suicide places survivors at increased risk for suicide. The suicide of a family member, particularly a parent, it is hypothesized, marks some survivors with a type of trauma associated with moral injury, which may produce an alteration in object relations with the emergence of what may be called a mysterious object. Under the press of these conditions, survivors may embark on what Apprey (2014) has termed an “urgent errand” in an effort to solve a problem in the anterior generation. Analysands with a history of familial suicide may bring symptoms of moral injury, a mysterious object relation, and a risk for suicide into the transference. The family history, life history, and literary work of the novelist Walker Percy, who had an extensive family history of suicide, provides evidence for the hypothesis linking moral injury, a mysterious object, and an urgent errand in such patients.

[[RunningHead]]

The Struggle for Supremacy in Wagner's Der Ring Des Nibelungen: The Breaking of the Father's Authority

Ilany Kogan

Ilany Kogan

2, Mohaliver Street
Rehovot
ISRAEL
E-mail: ilanyk@yahoo.com

Ilany Kogan   Training Analyst, Israel Psychoanalytic Society.

Wagner's music has the capacity to cast a spell over the listener, to transport him or her to realms of unimaginable ecstasy. Wagner's operas are unique in that he wrote not only the music, but also the libretto, thus inventing a new musical genre—the musical drama. The epic opera cycle Der Ring des Nibelungen (The Ring of the Nibelungs) is a musical drama that reflects the composer's attitude toward gods and subhumans, representing Aryans and Jews, respectively. The music as well as the plot and action of the opera may evoke in the listener archaic fears that may elicit a reaction either of repulsion or of great admiration. It is possible that these polar reactions parallel the split existing in Wagner himself regarding his attitude toward Aryans and Jews. I will examine that attitude as reflected in The Ring libretto and compare it to that expressed in his essays and letters.

The Plot

Wagner's opera cycle Der Ring des Nibelungen comprises four operas: Das Rheingold (first performance, Munich 1869), Die Walküre (first performance, Munich 1870), Siegfried (first performance, Bayreuth 1876), and Götterdämmerung (first performance, Bayreuth 1876). The story revolves around a magic ring that grants its owner the power to rule the world. The ring was forged by Alberich, a Nibelung dwarf, from gold stolen from the Rhinemaidens. With the assistance of the demigod Loge, Wotan, king of the gods, steals the ring from Alberich, but he is forced to hand it over to two giants, Fafner and Fasolt, as payment for building his fortress, Valhalla.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Group as Complicit in Boundary Violations: Commentary on Dimen

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

6750 West Loop S, Suite 460
Bellaire, TX 77401-4113
E-mail: glen.gabbard@gmail.com

Glen Gabbard  

In my thirty years of studying boundary violations in psychoanalysis, I have grown convinced that the approach to the problem is only partially addressed by exploring the individual issues in the analysts and the “fit” between analyst and patient in a particular dyad. Data of that kind are useful and can help us think about prevention at the level of the individual analyst. However, the analytic community's response to boundary violations has alerted me to the fact that there are powerful group forces operating, as Muriel Dimen has eloquently elaborated in her superb contribution to this literature. As she persuasively states, the discovery of a transgression evokes in others the unvoiced terror of being “outed” and consequently discredited for some secret personal truth of which they are ashamed. In my brief presentation I will draw heavily on Bion's ideas about groups as I share some of my observations as a consultant to analytic groups and as an evaluator and treater of individual analysts and patients who have been involved in sexual boundary violations.

There was once a deafening silence about the problem of sexual boundary violations in the psychoanalytic profession. In 1989 I served on the American Psychoanalytic Association Program Committee, and I proposed that we present a panel on the subject. I was told by a senior colleague that it wasn't necessary since “we already know all we need to know about the problem.” In May 1996 a COPE study group was organized within the association to study boundary violations in depth. Morris Peltz and I coauthored the first publication of that group (Gabbard and Peltz 2001).

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Baylor College of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, Center for Psychoanalytic Studies, Houston.

Stuart Twemlow

Stuart Twemlow

2466 SW Brookhaven Lane
Topeka, KA 66614
E-mail: stuart.twemlow@gmail.com

Stuart Twemlow   Visiting Professor, University College London (Health Sciences); Editor-in-Chief, International Journal of Applied Psychoanalytic Studies.

Although Mardi Horowitz's Identity and the New Psychoanalytic Explorations of Self-Organization runs a mere 125 pages, it is one of the most challenging psychoanalytic works I have been asked to review. I would far rather have written a short blurb for it, as it was praised unreservedly by two respected colleagues, the late Robert Wallerstein, who wrote the book's foreword, and Peter Fonagy. When I delved into the book, however, I found quite a few difficulties that I will try to outline here. Horowitz, a leader in the field of identity formation, takes an approach very different from Erik Erikson's, in spite of his adequate and respectful knowledge of Erikson's work.

responseSet.title: A Special Sort of Forgetting: Negation in Freud and Augustine
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Special Sort of Forgetting: Negation in Freud and Augustine

Daniel Rosengart

Daniel Rosengart

45-34 42nd Street
Queens, NY 11104
E-mail: daniel@rosengart.com

Daniel Rosengart  

Freud, Saint Augustine, Pseudo-Dionysius, forgetting, negation

The development from a positive, dualistic theory of memory and forgetting to a concept of memory that includes forgetting as a variety of remembering is traced in two thinkers. Freud's concept of repression is discussed as a complex negation of both remembering and forgetting, and the development of this construct is shown in his letters to Wilhelm Fliess. A close reading of Augustine of Hippo's Confessions shows a similar concept of a special sort of forgetting, in which what is forgotten is remembered nonetheless. Finally, the limits of the comparison are discussed, and a reading of Freud's “Negation” reveals ways in which the unconscious is fundamentally unlike Augustine's interiority.

Clinical supervision of psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Edited by Jill Savege Scharff. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xvi + 176 pp., $39.95 paperback

Daniel Jacobs

Daniel Jacobs

64 Williston Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: danielhjacobs1@gmail.com

Daniel Jacobs   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Florida Psychoanalytic Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School; Director, Center for Advanced Psychoanalytic Studies, Princeton.

It is difficult to say anything new about the task of supervision. The same shibboleths find their way into print: supervision is not psychotherapy; empathy with and sensitivity to the supervisee is essential, as is awareness of the tension between the need to supervise and the need to evaluate. Discuss learning goals at the beginning of supervision. Be aware of parallel processes. While such reminders are helpful, they do not go far enough. For reasons of confidentiality, there is often a paucity of in-depth supervisory process reported, Dewald's book (1987) being a notable exception. Detailed descriptions of trainees’ difficulties in learning are understandably kept out of the literature, lest supervisees or their patients be identified. It is a relief, then, to find several chapters in Jill Savege Scharff's edited collection that describe in some detail the supervisor's challenge in trying to reach students whose ability to use supervision seems limited. Carl Bagnini (“Supervision or Thera-Vision? Working with Unconscious Motives in the Supervisory Encounter”) writes of an older experienced supervisee who “wanted reward for talents not achieved” and could not subject herself to being “a clinical supervisee” (p. 51). Scharff (“Supervision in the Learning Matrix”) describes a therapist who was unable to share her emotional responses to the clinical material she presented and was often unaware of the transference implications of her patient's communications. Ending the collection on a positive note, Scharff tells how over time she successfully helped her supervisee become more aware of her patient's feelings, as well as her own responses to them, without becoming her therapist. Other contributions include a sensitive and moving account of Elizabeth Thomas's relationally based research on conflicts between supervisor and supervisee.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Aspects of Assisted Reproductive Technology. Edited by Mali Mann. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xxxiv +119 pp., $29.95 paperback.

Gabriela Legorreta

Gabriela Legorreta

603 Roslyn Avenue
Westmount, QC H3Y 2V1
CANADA
E-mail: <mariagabrielalegorreta@gmail.com>

Gabriela Legorreta   Training and Supervising Analyst, Montreal Psychoanalytic Society; Consultant, Montreal Fertility Centre.

In this edited collection Mali Mann provides the reader an in-depth exploration of the complex psychic experiences of individuals dealing with infertility and attempting to conceive using reproductive technology. The varied cases presented here highlight the importance of exploring the unconscious conflicts and fantasies awakened in both parents and children before, during, and after such conceptions. The vast landscape of themes she and her contributors explore includes issues around female and masculine identity; questions surrounding repetition compulsion in the treatment of infertility; the role of donors in the fantasy life of parents and children; the struggle with securing parental identity formation; ambivalence about motherhood; and the long-term effects on patients resorting to reproductive assistance. The presentation of moving case histories interwoven with these theoretical questions makes this book a worthwhile, original contribution to the complex and timely subject of reproductive technology. It is little wonder that this work received a Pinnacle Achievement Award from the National Association of Book Entrepreneurs in 2015 as best book in the category of parenting and family.

The book consists of two parts. The first offers a wide exploration of clinical perspectives. The second, a single concluding chapter, brings together in a coherent whole the major subjects addressed in the book.

The first chapter, written by Mann herself, invites the reader to consider, through the comceptual lens of repetition compulsion, the experience of repeated in vitro fertilization trials and failures. In some cases, the author argues, repetitive attempts to conceive through IVF can be understood as a repetition compulsion of earlier repressed trauma. Describing how women are profoundly affected by fertility failure, Mann stresses that the experience of infertility brings into question the identificatory link to the primary maternal object, thereby threatening self-representation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Chen Yu & Linda B. Smith (2016). The social origins of sustained attention in one-year-old human infants.Current Biology26:1235-1240.

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz  

Attention, a cognitive capacity that is important in its own right, is an integral aspect of other cognitive capacities such as perception and memory. The ability to select one part of the complex stimuli in one's environment is necessary for survival. Even more critical is the ability to sustain attention, since without sustained attention there can be no learning.

In a recent study, Chen Yu and Linda Smith (Indiana University-Bloomington) address how this critical capacity arises and what factors influence its development. Specifically, these researchers ask: Does the capacity for sustained attention arise through maturation alone, pari passu, along with other cognitive capacities, or does particular environmental input facilitate its development? As the title of their article states, they found significant effects of specific caregiver behaviors on an infant's capacity for sustained attention. According to a press release from Indiana University, theirs is the “first study to show a direct connection between how long a caregiver looks at an object and how long an infant's attention remains focused on that same object.”

Their study population consisted of 36 infant-parent dyads (19 male infants, 17 female, ages 11 to 13 months) engaged in free-flowing interactions with three toys (pretested as engaging and novel).

Jeffrey Rubin

Jeffrey Rubin

1841 Broadway, Suite 711
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: jeffreyrubin@optonline.net

Jeffrey Rubin   Private practice of psychoanalysis and analytically oriented therapy, New York; teacher of meditation, New York and Bedford Hills, New York.

There's a burgeoning interest in the integration of the Eastern meditative and Western psychotherapeutic traditions among mental professionals, their patients, researchers, and spiritual practitioners. Increasing numbers of people meditate, and meditators—including Buddhist teachers—participate in psychoanalysis and psychotherapy.

Studying psychoanalysis and Buddhism in tandem might seem like a shotgun wedding. What, after all, could an ethical psychology developed in the fifth century b.c. in India for attaining spiritual enlightenment and liberation have in common with a theory and treatment that arose in Europe in the late nineteenth century to address psychopathology and emotional distress? Psychoanalysis uses a self-reflective relationship to explore how formative unconscious experiences and conflicts from the past affect and restrict our sense of self and relationships in the present, while Buddhism, especially Zen, is skeptical about the validity of concepts and words and offers a practice that fosters the capacity to let go of attachments so as to awaken to a fuller experience of self, have a more intimate relationship to the world, and act with greater moral wisdom.

But as we look more carefully, commonalities—even synergies—emerge. Both disciplines are concerned with lessening anguish and shedding light on what it means to be human. Each has a diagnosis of what afflicts us, as well as a “treatment plan.” And both emphasize cultivating nonjudgmental awareness, which can aid us in living more wholeheartedly and experiencing greater intimacy and meaning. Practitioners of each discipline are using—even blending—insights and practices from both, leading to mutual enrichment (see, e.g., Rubin 1996, 1999, 2009, 2013; Magid 2002; Safran 2003; Young-Eisendrath 2003; Engler 2003; Akhtar 2005; Cooper 2007; Bobrow 2010; Finn and Rubin 2014). For example, therapists who meditate report with consistency that it cultivates greater self-awareness, compassion, and wisdom.

[[RunningHead]]

Analytic Controversy

Le Psychanalyste apathique et le patient postmoderne (The Apathetic Psychoanalyst and the Postmodern Patient). By Laurence Kahn. Paris: Éditions de l'Olivier, 2014, 172 pp., €18.00 paperback.

Wilfried Eecke

Wilfried Eecke

4100 Nebraska Avenue, NW
Washington, DC 20016
E-mail: vereeckw@georgetown.edu

Wilfried Eecke   Professor of Philosophy, Georgetown University; Advanced Candidate, Contemporary Freudian Society.

Psychoanalysis, particularly in the United States, has tried to make itself acceptable. In doing so, argues the French analyst Laurence Kahn, it has diminished what we might call Freud's “shocking emphasis” on the economy of the drives and in particular on sexuality. And instead of accepting Freud's “cold,” interpretive attitude, postmodern psychoanalysts have replaced it with empathy for the patient. To accommodate itself to postmodernism, psychoanalysis went a step further and in many quarters accepted the idea that there are no impartial psychoanalytic observations or valid interpretations. Instead of aspiring to such knowledge, postmodern analysts opted to view their work as providing hermeneutic understanding. In The Apathetic Psychoanalyst and the Postmodern Patient, Kahn offers as proof of her thesis the fact that in American psychoanalysis the power of language to unearth secrets, or of silence to break through superficialities, gives way to three other ideas: the idea of integration, the idea of the internal world (which replaces the notion of the unconscious), and the idea of the “self” (a word that has become omnipresent).

The book ends with a series of informative one-page descriptions of about fifty psychoanalysts and philosophers, from both sides of the Atlantic, who have influenced postmodern psychoanalysis.

Early on the author quotes Peter Fonagy, who rejects the idea that the transference is the mobilization of repressed infantile forces. Instead Fonagy sees the transference as making visible the attitude of “I-with-another,” which then in analysis can be addressed and corrected in the “here-and-now.” Hence, psychoanalysis is work that assumes a two-person psychology.

This leads naturally to stressing in the analyst the need for empathy, an emphasis Paul Ricoeur has remarked in his writings on psychoanalysis. Instead of offering Freudian interpretation, postmodern analysts feel they have to help, support, and respond to their patients.

871
