[[RunningHead]]

Religious Coping and Defense Style in a Sample of Persons with Cognitive Complaints

Leila Abuelhiga , Kevin Mazzola , Lana Tiersky , Benjamin Freer , Evelina Eyzerovich , Michelle Pievsky and Usha Persaud

Usha Persaud

1000 River Road T-WHI-01
Teaneck, NJ 07666
E-mail: labuelh1@student.fdu.edu

Usha Persaud  

In the face of life stressors, both defense style and coping strategies represent attempts to help reduce negative affect and maintain an individual's psychological equilibrium (Cramer 2008). Several studies have demonstrated the positive effects of the use of coping strategies (especially religious ones) and mature defenses on life satisfaction and psychological adjustment, particularly in persons with psychiatric disabilities and physical illnesses (Eriksen, Olff, and Ursin 1997; Flannery and Perry 1990; Olff, Brosschot, and Godaert, 1993; Malone et al. 2013; Sepidehdam, Karimi, and Besharat 2012; Valliant and Drake 1985; Albuquerque et al. 2011; Warren et al. 2015; Rosmarin et al. 2013). Despite such findings, no research directly comparing defense styles and religious coping strategies used in adults seeking mental health services currently exists. The present study examined the relationship between defense style and religious coping in a population of patients with cognitive complaints.

Coping strategies and defense styles are not synonymous; indeed, they are distinct constructs. Unlike defenses, which are viewed as unconscious and reflexive processes in nature, coping is viewed as a conscious, effortful process (Cramer 2000). In addition, coping strategies are thought to be activated by external stressors, whereas defenses are thought to be evoked by an underlying internal conflict (Bouchard and Thériault 2003). Despite these differences, a relationship does exist between the two constructs. Defensive responses seem to precede coping responses.

Psychoanalytic Process

The Play within the Play: The Enacted Dimension of Psychoanalytic Process. By Gil Katz. New York: Routledge, 2014, xxii + 185 pp., $180.00 hardcover, $55.95 paperback.

Eugene Mahon

Eugene Mahon

8 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: ejmahon8@gmail.com

Eugene Mahon   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; member, Center for Advanced Psychoanalytic Studies.

In the soliloquy in act 2, scene 2, when Hamlet first stumbles on the idea of the play within the play, he muses to himself: “I have heard / that guilty creatures sitting at a play / have by the very cunning of the scene / been struck so to the soul that presently / they have proclaimed their malefactions / since murder though it have no tongue, will speak / with most miraculous organ….” In this engaging, thought-provoking book Gil Katz suggests that this most miraculous, tongueless organ can be compared most fruitfully to what he calls the enacted dimension of psychoanalytic process. Katz, like Shakespeare, is suggesting that the unconscious can be “outed” through other than linguistic pathways: it can “speak” through the miraculous organ of enactment. In fact, when Hamlet stages the play within the play a guilty Claudius “speaks” nonverbally: he leaves the theater abruptly, his wordless guilt announcing itself in a dramatic enactment. Katz uses the concept of the play within the play to suggest a double-tiered complexity that informs the drama of analytic process: its language component and its enacted dimension.

For thirty or more years this “most miraculous organ,” this enacted dimension of therapeutic process has engaged the science of mental conflict (as Brenner called psychoanalysis), as a new way of thinking about the complexities of analysis and how its mysterious ministry works. The classical model suggested that once the fundamental rule had been announced to a cooperative analysand, the analyst's evenly hovering attention would monitor the flow of free associations as the unconscious gradually became more and more “comfortable” revealing itself to both parties of the analytic dyad.

精神分析师的欲望和自恋性阻抗问题

米切爾 威爾遜

米切爾 威爾遜

米切爾 威爾遜  

(THE ANALYST S DESIRE AND THE PROBLEM OF NARCISSISTIC RESISTANCES)

分析师不可避免地会欲求着与病人的特别体验，这常常导向自恋性基础上的阻抗。这样的欲望形式是值得思考的。可以检视一下这五种假设： 1 分析师情不自禁地会有欲望，并想要被分析者认可这些欲望； 2 这些欲望经常标识了分析师的理论信仰和技术性干预； 3 自恋性的欲望及其影响在分析实践中是普遍存在的； 4 病人经常在留心分析师的各种目的； 5 病人常常希望分析师将他的欲望放在一 边来倾听自己，以使病人能够深入到自己的利益中去。拉康“二元关系”的概念是这一讨论的核心。新克莱因派关于自恋性阻抗的位置也将被探究，而“分析性第三者”的概念也成为了这一问题的一 个潜在解决方式。由此扩展的一个案例阐明了这些主要观点。

有时她只是距离的面具，消失的面具，

她让你说话，

让你自由地说出你的意思而无须立刻

在每个词上押上你的人生。

那么多的地下室磁带是最纯粹的自由言说：

单纯的自由言说，

平凡的自由言说，

无意义的自由言说，

[[RunningHead]]

JAPA Prize Announcement

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $500 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

Henry Friedman   Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

The powerful impact of classical psychoanalytic theory on the practice of psychoanalysis in the United States rested initially upon its presentation as a scientific study of the mind, in health and disturbance, particularly with regard to manifestations of unconscious processes. The appeal of Freud's scientific certainty to psychiatrists in the United States related not only to their medical training but also to the sense that they were discovering a new approach to people suffering from emotional distress and illnesses. Psychoanalysis, while introduced into the United States in the 1920s, blossomed into the dominant therapeutic modality in the 1950s and 1960s; in those decades, which marked the height of “therapeutic optimism,” it was seen as having potential for treating a broad range of patients, ranging from functional “neurotics” suffering from “internal conflict” to psychotic patients diagnosed with major mental illnesses.

Dawn Skorczewski

Dawn Skorczewski

200 Main Street
Waltham, MA 02149
E-mail: <dawnskor@brandeis.edu>

Dawn Skorczewski   Professor of English and Director of University Writing, Brandeis University.

When I first heard the booming voice of Donald Antrim reading from The Emerald Light in the Air in an Artist/Scholar in Residence session at the APsaA meetings last year, I decided that every reader should have the experience of hearing a supremely gifted writer reading his story exactly as he meant it to be read. How better to learn the craft of making meaning on the page than by hearing how a writer makes his?

As I gazed around at the rapt audience of psychoanalysts, it seemed to me that their appreciation was palpable. The question and answer session focused on how Antrim's experiences as a mental patient figure in his fictional characters' struggles with grief, paranoia, and florid psychosis. Would it be possible, I wondered, for Antrim's appeal to psychoanalysts to extend to the psychoanalytic classroom?

As an affiliate scholar at the Boston Psychoanalytic Institute over a decade ago, I found the experience of listening to analytic material in technique and case seminars almost always exhilarating. Each class session offered a new world of texts and contexts, and theories with which to think about them. I listened as an English professor, bringing the tools of interpretation from my own discipline to the study of human texts.

I thought more than once about Herman Melville's Bartleby the Scrivener in these seminars. Humbled by my own fears and limited knowledge of the field, I wondered if I was an anxious Bartleby, preferring not to learn from the cases themselves but instead standing in line to show how much I already knew about the subject. Others joined me, and sometimes an entire class left the patient's story to the side as they searched for answers to the most difficult problems.

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: <arniedr15@gmail.com>

Arnold Richards   Former Editor, Journal of the American Psychoanalytic-Association; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

How does the reviewer approach a book with the title War is Not Inevitable when he believes it is? The book's subtitle, On the Psychology of War and Aggression, broaches a subject about which much can be written, and that will be a focus of my review. Vamik Volkan, in his introduction, states that Henri Parens's main reason for writing the book is to tell us Freud was wrong in his response to Einstein's question: Why war? Does psychoanalysis have any new insights that might deliver us from the scourge of war? Parens points out that Freud based his response on his death instinct theory, which Parens rightly calls into question, as have many others since Freud offered that metapsychological point of view. Melanie Klein in Europe and Karl Menninger in the United States were mostly in agreement with Freud on this matter, but others, particularly Otto Kernberg, have had more nuanced views on the subject.

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian

Quebec G1W 2C1
1180 Murray Avenuez

Marie-Ange Pongis-Khandjian   Psychologist and psychoanalyst, Quebec Psychoanalytic Society; Secretary, Canadian Psychoanalytic Society.

In this book divided in three parts—theoretical, clinical, and personal— Samuel Arbiser, an Argentinian training analyst, gathers papers he has written between 1972 and 2012. Together they provide a panoramic view of the development of psychoanalysis in the region of Rio de la Plata from the 1950s to the 1980s.

The author describes an early psychoanalytic movement in Argentina characterized by a Kleinian hegemony. This was followed by a period of theoretical and technical pluralism marked by French psychoanalytic thinking, particularly that of Lacan. While authors like Enrique Pichon-Rivière, José Bleger, David Liberman, Willy and Madeleine Baranger, and Horacio Etchegoyen were influenced by these currents of thought, they developed their own innovative and enriching concepts.

Always referring primarily to Freud, Arbiser weaves a fruitful and lively dialogue between his teachers and predecessors and his own clinical experience. Throughout the chapters, he shows that it is possible to be open to new ideas while remaining faithful to one's roots.

Arbiser allows us to discover the vitality of Argentinian psychoanalysis, especially through the ideas of Pichon-Rivière, of whom he was particularly fond. This Swiss-born analyst was the founder of psychoanalysis in Argentina. Creative freethinker, inexhaustible innovator without a dogmatic straitjacket, with multiple and diverse interests (artistic, sporting, political, social) and with a scientific, psychosocial, and multidisciplinary worldview, he was little known in Europe and North America. His fertile creativity did not always coincide with his written work. Because for the most part he taught orally, in seminars and lectures, his seminal ideas could be found only piecemeal in various writings.

David Solomon

David Solomon

441 East Erie Street, Suite 4304

David Solomon   Council Member, International Association for Psychoanalytic Self Psychology.

A good autobiography or memoir, like any good story, allows us to relate to the author emotionally, share his skin, and understand the forces at work in his life. We immerse ourselves empathically over a sustained period.

We want to know how the world looks from inside the author's experience, and when that need is met by a convincing narrative, it is deeply satisfying and illuminating.

Autobiography is not really “about” the facts and events related; it is about how the author chooses to interpret and make sense of these events. We are drawn to the tensions and the drama of the struggle to make meaning and to arrive at self-knowledge. We are interested in understanding the self, and the psychological, social, and political forces that affect that self.

Life is a journey through trials and tribulations, where to succeed one must have courage, endurance, cunning, and moral strength. But that's getting ahead of the story.

Christian Maetzener

950 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10028

Christian Maetzener   Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine; Adjunct Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

There is an inscription on the temple of Isis in Sais, Egypt, that reads: “I am all that has been, that is, and that shall be; no mortal has yet raised my veil.” Throughout history, that veil has been an invitation for philosophers, historians, and poets to find answers and delve further into the truth. The eighteenth-century German poet Friedrich Schiller (1795) ends his poem about a young man who in his search for wisdom tries to lift the veil:

Whate'er he saw, whatever then he learned

His lips have never told: but gone for aye

Was all the former gladness of his life,

And sorrow bore him to an early grave.

“Woe be to him,” his warning voice would say

When urgent questioners around him pressed,

“Woe be to him who seeks for Truth through sin!

For Truth so found no happiness will yield.”

We analysts are aware of our struggle with what we see and learn in one particular area: aggression—not only manifestations of aggression inside the consulting room but also the effects of aggression directed from without at us, our patients, and our work.

Selected Poster Summaries from the 2016 Poster Session of the American Psychoanalytic Association

The Relationship between Defense Style and Intelligence

Michelle Pievsky , Usha Persaud , Lana Tiersky , Benjamin Freer , Leila Abuelhiga , Kevin Mazzola and Evelina Eyzerovich

Evelina Eyzerovich

1000 River Road, T-WH1-01
Teaneck, NJ 07666
E-mail: mpievsky@student.fdu.edu.

Evelina Eyzerovich  

The aim of this study was to examine the relationship between intelligence and defense style, as measured by the Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale, Third and Fourth Editions (WAIS-III, IV; Wechsler 1997a, 2008a) and the 40-item Defense Style Questionnaire (DSQ-40; Andrews, Pollock, and Stewart 1989). Several studies have evaluated the relationship between intelligence and defense mechanisms, with mixed results, and no studies have looked at the relationship between intelligence and defense style, as defined by any version of the Defense Style Questionnaire.

Defense mechanisms are an important aspect of development and ego functioning (A. Freud 1936). Many factors contribute to an individual's level of ego functioning, including intelligence. Several studies have found that participants with a higher IQ exhibit greater ego functioning (Allen, Coyne, and David 1986; Spivack, Levine, and Sprigle 1959). The relationship between intelligence and defense style, however, has been less clearly established.

Haan (1963) was the first to study the relationship between intelligence and defense mechanisms, using data from the Terman Group Test. She proposed that coping (e.g.

Joseph D. Lichtenberg

Psychoanalytic Theory, Research, and Clinical Practice: Reading Joseph D. Lichtenberg. Edited by Linda Gunsberg and Sandra G. Hershberg. New York: Routledge, 2016, xx + 322 pp., $190.00 hardcover, $52.95 paperback.

Henry Friedman

Henry Friedman

6 Garden Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: friedmanhj@aol.com

Henry Friedman   Associate Professor of Psychiatry (part-time), Harvard Medical School.

This is an unusual book about a unique individual, Joe Lichtenberg, who has lived a long and productive life as a psychoanalyst, educator, clinician, and theoretician. As someone who has known him for over thirty-five years and considers him a good friend, I feel a certain pride and privilege in being able to review this volume. During last year Joe turned ninety, a milestone celebrated with a conference in his honor in Washington, D.C., where he lives and works. The conference paid tribute to the many contributions he has made to psychoanalysis. Joe Lichtenberg has operated in his energetic way to influence the direction of psychoanalysis, for analysts of all theoretical persuasions but mainly as someone who has contributed deeply to self psychology, both theoretically and organizationally. In the world of psychoanalysis, one grows used to reading volumes honoring important contributors to our field; typically, they consist of papers written by the honoree's close colleagues, papers inspired by the seminal work of the analyst, who is presented as having made important contributions to our field. Psychoanalytic Theory, Research and Clinical Practice: Reading Joseph D. Lichtenberg deviates from this tradition, as was the intention of its editors, Linda Gunsburg and Sandra G. Hershberg, both of them close colleagues and friends of Lichtenberg. They have achieved their goal of presenting a full picture of their subject, a combination of biography and studies of his contributions to psychoanalytic theory and practice. They achieve a remarkable result by including the full texts of interviews conducted with Joe and his wife Charlotte over a period of months. These five interviews make up the book's first section.

A Murder over a Girl: Justice, Gender, Junior High. By Ken Corbett. New York: Henry Holt, 2016, xiv + 273 pp., $27.00 hardcover.

Bart Magee

Bart Magee

459 Fulton Street, Suite 202
San Francisco, CA 94102
E-mail: bartmagee@gmail.com

Bart Magee   Executive Director, Access Institute for Psychological Services; member, Division 39 (psychoanalysis) of the American Psychological Association.

A Murder over a Girl, Ken Corbett's artful Russian nesting dolls of a book (a junior high tragedy inside a psychoanalytic character study inside a story about boys—gender-variant and gender-panicked—inside a courtroom drama), breaks new ground as a psychoanalytic work. It also provides inspiration to those of us who recognize the value of a psychoanalytic perspective not only in therapy, but also outside, in the public sphere. Corbett tells the profoundly moving and tragic story of a fifteen-year-old California boy who had recently begun to identify as transgender and was murdered at school by a male classmate. In writing the book, Corbett immersed himself deeply in his subject, not only attending the entire murder trial, but also interviewing key participants extensively and throughout the ordeal. Having done so, he is able to contextualize the human story within the social, cultural, and gender dramas surrounding it. Though the incident occurred eight years ago, the book, considering the current social and political climate, could not be more timely.

Like Corbett's previous book, Boyhoods: Rethinking Masculinities, this one is highly theoretical. Yet it communicates subtle and sophisticated thoughts in clear language and an emotionally present voice that is intimate to the experience of the two teenaged boys, one dead, the other accused of the murder, and to all the people he meets or observes: students, family, community members, lawyers, witnesses, jurors. While staying close to his subjects, Corbett inserts his own measured thinking on the psychological and social themes the story evokes. Child development, gender, psychopathology, trauma, family relations, and sociocultural dynamics are all attentively explored and interwoven.

In its first pages, he presents a key courtroom scene in which a teenage witness to the murder takes the stand; through a sensitive accounting, Corbett immediately provides an emotional closeness to his subjects. His evocation of the scene allows the reader to experience what can be called “psychoanalytic listening,” that is, attending carefully and simultaneously to everything in the interpersonal and social environment.

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association

JAPA Prize

The $500 JAPA Prize is awarded to the author of the best paper published in the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association during the previous year. All publications, including review articles, research articles, essays and book essays, may be considered for the award. Candidates for the award are nominated by the members of the JAPA Editorial Board, with the final decision being made by the Editor-in-Chief.

JAPA New Author Prize

JAPA's $500 New Author Prize recognizes significant contributions to the journal by new authors. It is awarded for the best published manuscript by a new author, and is presented at the Winter Meetings of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

A new author is considered to be someone who has not previously published in JAPA or any other major psychoanalytic journal and whose psychoanalytic career is in its early stages. A mentoring/editorial group has been established to review submissions by new authors and assist in developing manuscripts for publication in JAPA.

Manuscripts must be submitted via the JAPA submission process, using the online system at http://mc.manuscriptcentral.com/japa. At the time of submission, authors should indicate in the cover letter that the paper is to be considered for the New Author Prize, and may ask that it be directed to the mentoring/editorial group for assistance in manuscript development. Final decision about publication remains with the Editorial Board and the Editor-in-Chief, as does selection of the annual prize-winning paper.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Negative Self-Evaluating Emotions as Mediator in the Relationship between Childhood Emotional Trauma and Alexithymia in Adulthood

Serge Lecours , Frédérick Philippe , Marie-Ève Boucher , Lola Ahoundova and Catherine Allard-Chapais

Catherine Allard-Chapais

C.P. 6128, succ. Centre-ville
Montreal, Quebec, H3C 3J7
CANADA
E-mail: serge.lecours@umontreal.ca

Catherine Allard-Chapais  

Alexithymia, a cognitive deficit in the experience of emotion, is associated with low mentalizing and emotional dysregulation (Taylor and Bagby 2013). Empirically, alexithymia has been associated with childhood trauma and the experience of negative emotions in adulthood (Kooiman, et al. 2004; Paivio and McCulloch 2004). However, research findings about these relationships are too sketchy and general to inspire a specific causal model for the development of alexithymia. How does trauma lead to difficulty in identifying and expressing emotions? What do negative emotions tell us about the deficits in emotion regulation at the heart of alexithymia?

Background

The original formulation of alexithymia, stemming from psychoanalytic observation of psychosomatic patients, has emphasized a deficit in mentalization (Taylor, Bagby, and Parker 1997). Trauma has long been thought to be an etiological factor contributing to alexithymia. In such a view, alexithymia is seen as a defense or adaptation to trauma, more or less associated with dissociation (Krystal 1988). Trauma is seen mostly as a unitary “quantitative” phenomenon, and alexithymia is conceived as dealing with an emotional overflow. However, studies have shown that some types of trauma, more emotionally based, might be more closely related to alexithymia (Paivio and McCulloch 2004). They point to etiological mechanisms more directly related to the regulation of emotion.

Richard Tuch

Richard Tuch

1800 Fairburn Avenue, Suite 206
Los Angeles, CA 90025
E-mail: rtuch@aol.com

Richard Tuch   Former Dean of Training, New Center for Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles; Training and Supervising Analyst, New Center for Psychoanalysis and the Psychoanalytic Center of California; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA.

What becomes apparent as one makes one's way through this collection of well-written and well-reasoned papers, chapters, and book reviews written or co-written by Arnold Richards is the fact that Richards has a bone to pick with analysts who make a habit of dismissing, to the point of condemning, the work of colleagues whose approach is largely informed by modern conflict theory. What particularly galls Richards is the way in which these critics unkindly, unfairly, and reductionistically misrepresent the work of contemporary Freudians, which they see as singularly mechanistic, drive-oriented, oedipally focused, and conflict-obsessed while touting their own “evolved” approach as being more humanistic, caring, experience-near, and antiauthoritarian.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Levy and Finnegan

Peter Dunn

Peter Dunn

241 East 82nd Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: pbdunnmd@gmail.com

Peter Dunn  

This case report is organized around a series of vivid, emotionally evocative dreams. Read sequentially in chronological order, the dreams amount to Anne's narrative of the treatment, a parallel case report written by the patient in the language of the dream.

A significant obstacle to comparative psychoanalysis is that the information of most importance to analysts of one school is often missing in the clinical material from another. Ego psychologists approach the understanding of a dream by tracking the patient's associations and by identifying the day residue. Anne's dreams are presented with relatively little of the information that ego psychologists rely on, but the authors give detailed descriptions of the enacted transference-countertransference and describe the interactions between patient and analyst as they worked on each dream. This information is of most use in understanding dreams in which the patient's wish to communicate something to the analyst about the analysis is a dynamically important element of the latent content. In what follows I will focus on five of Anne's dreams that are of this type. Such dreams often transparently represent the patient's experience of the self in the analysis and relational scenarios that are perceptions of self-with-the-analyst; clearly the patient intends for the analyst to grasp the dream's meaning.

Anne

Anne is the daughter of parents who were overwhelmed with the demands of caring for her violent, mentally ill older brother. She describes her parents as having little energy to parent her and as valuing her mainly for helping them with the brother.

Director of Clinical Services, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

[[RunningHead]]

Fairbairn and the Object Relations Tradition. Edited by Graham S. Clarke and David E. Scharff. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xxxiv + 519 pp., $80.95 paperback.

Monisha Nayar-Akhtar

Monisha Nayar-Akhtar

300 East Lancaster Avenue, Suite 201-B
Wynnewood, PA 19096
E-mail: monisha_akhtar@hotmail.com

Monisha Nayar-Akhtar   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia; Founder, Indian Institute of Psychotherapy Training, New Delhi.

W. R. D. Fairbairn: “the man who un-split the psychic atom.” This opening line, by series editors Norka Malberg and Joan Raphael-Leff in their foreword to this volume, captures succinctly the momentous transformative power of Fairbairn's theoretical and technical contributions to psychoanalysis. Fairbairn, a quiet Scotsman, developed his ideas in the 1940s, though he remained relatively unknown during his lifetime. That well-known fact is substantially documented throughout the book. Fairbairn's rise to fame occurred gradually and propitiously with the support of a small band of followers who expanded on his ideas and lent credibility to his psychoanalytic conceptualizations. Beginning with Henry Gunthrip, who in the 1970s precipitated this movement, recognition of the impact of Fairbairn's ideas on psychoanalytic clinical practice and theory has been growing. This voluminous compilation draws on the growing ranks of Fairbairn's admirers, and its editors, Graham Clarke and David Scharff, are to be commended for their efforts.

The book is part of the Lines of Development series launched in 2012 with the support of Karnac Books. A collaborative intellectual enterprise, the series memorializes giants in the field of psychoanalysis. To date it includes four books, with a promise of many more to come. This volume on Fairbairn, the second in the series, stands tall and sturdy between Anna Freud and Winnicott (the fourth is dedicated to Bion). It canonizes a man who lived in relative obscurity, with a decidedly philosophical and theological bent, who worked quietly with populations that reflected his vast and diverse clinical interests, and whose sociopolitical interests were often reflected in his work. A compilation of Fairbairn's papers (1952) emerged from his clinical practice, papers that redefined the nature of ego functions, revolutionizing and breaking away from prevailing models of psychic functioning. Anyone familiar with the object relations tradition will find him- or herself on familiar ground in reading the present book. Indeed, the leitmotif of the volume is its emphatic avowal of Fairbairn's ideas, then and now, and his continual presence in our psychoanalytic minds and lives.

Journal Watch

Rainer Leyh, Christine Heinisch, Johanna Behringer, Iris Reiner, & Gottfried Spangler (2016). Maternal attachment representation and neurophysiological processing during the perception of infants' emotional expressions.PLoS ONE11(2), February 10.

Estephanie Bravo , Robert Melara and Eric Fertuck

Eric Fertuck

Eric Fertuck  

At the University of Erlangen-Nuremberg, Rainer Leyh, Christine Heinisch, Johanna Behringer, Iris Reiner, and Gottfried Spangler have taken a neurophysiological approach to studying maternal attachment in new mothers. Their approach includes a psychoanalytic perspective as well: the researchers examine how mothers' experiences shape their personalities and in turn their ability to understand their children's needs.

John Bowbly's attachment theory proposes that children begin to exhibit attachment behavior when confronted with changes in their environment, be it new people, new locations, or new objects. Van Rosmalen, van der Horst, and van der Veer (2016) state that “human infants need a consistent nurturing relationship with one or more sensitive caregivers to develop into healthy individuals” (p. 22). Children will develop different types of attachment patterns based on the sensitivity of their caretaker, usually the mother.

Mary Ainsworth further advanced attachment theory. She identified the different types of attachment a child can develop (Ainsworth et al. 1978). If the mother is able to meet her child's needs, the child will show either secure or insecure attachment. Children exhibiting secure attachment trust that their mother will respond to their needs; a mother will be available to them, and they will explore their environment if a new one is presented. By contrast, insecure children will either be clingy or will not seek out their mother. Often this occurs because the mother does not adequately respond to the child's needs. An insecure child will at times out of fear not explore the environment or will seek to explore it independently. A securely attached parent will be able to meet the child's needs and the child will feel safe knowing this. Insecurely attached parents will either be unavailable to tend to their child's needs or will meet them inadequately.

[[RunningHead]]

Changes in Self-Representations following Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy for Young Adults: A Comparative Typology

Andrzej Werbart , Lars Brusell , Rebecka Iggedal , Kristin Lavfors and Alexander Widholm

Alexander Widholm

SE-106 91 Stockholm
SWEDEN
E-mail: andrzej.werbart@psychology.su.se, Andrzej@Werbart.se

Alexander Widholm  

self-representations, young adults, ideal-type analysis, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, long-term follow-up, maturational process

Changes in dynamic psychological structures are often a treatment goal in psychotherapy. The present study aimed at creating a typology of self-representations among young women and men in psychoanalytic psychotherapy, to study longitudinal changes in self-representations, and to compare self-representations in the clinical sample with those of a nonclinical group. Twenty-five women and sixteen men were interviewed according to Blatt's Object Relations Inventory pretreatment, at termination, and at a 1.5-year follow-up. In the comparison group, eleven women and nine men were interviewed at baseline, 1.5 years, and three years later. Typologies of the 123 self-descriptions in the clinical group and 60 in the nonclinical group were constructed by means of ideal-type analysis for men and women separately. Clusters of self-representations could be depicted on a two-dimensional matrix with the axes Relatedness-Self-definition and Integration-Nonintegration. In most cases, the self-descriptions changed over time in terms of belonging to different ideal-type clusters. In the clinical group, there was a movement toward increased integration in self-representations, but above all toward a better balance between relatedness and self-definition. The changes continued after termination, paralleled by reduced symptoms, improved functioning, and higher developmental levels of representations. No corresponding tendency could be observed in the nonclinical group.

[[RunningHead]]

The One and the Many: Relational Psychoanalysis and Group Analysis. By Juan Tubert-Oklander. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xx + 295 pp., $39.95 paperback.

David Scharff

David Scharff

6612 Kennedy Drive
Chevy Chase, MD 20815
E-mail: davidscharff@theipi.org

David Scharff   Supervising Analyst, International Institute for Psychoanalytic Training at the International Psychotherapy Institute; Chair of the Board, International Psychotherapy Institute; Teaching Analyst, Washington Center for Psychoanalysis.

Juan Tubert-Oklander, a Mexican psychoanalyst and group analyst, is a polyglot, fortunately for us at home in several languages. Although he is

237

a native Spanish speaker, this book is not a translation. Written in English, it reads essentially as if the work of a native English speaker. The One and the Many is a treatise on what modern psychoanalysis can learn from the tradition of group analysis that flows equally from Enrique Pichon-Rivière in Argentina and S. H. Foulkes in Great Britain. The lessons to be learned from this perspective—radically different, to be sure, though germinated in part from seeds planted by Freud's writing on groups—can enrich psychoanalytic theory and practice (the latter only incidentally). Fairbairn's writing on the inner structures of object and ego pairs ripened in the little-known thought of Pichon-Rivière in Buenos Aires and Foulkes in London. They saw that the individual does not develop sui generis out of a primary narcissism but is from the beginning embedded in a relational matrix. This led to their view of people as constituted by two poles: at one pole individuals are autonomous, each with an independent inner world, while at the other they are continually constituted by relationships to which they reciprocally contribute. Taking this shift in perspective as its starting point, the group analytic perspective informs the intensely intrapsychic one that until recently has dominated psychoanalytic thought.

Psychoanalysts have largely ignored the social field. Traditional training, in emphasizing the analytic dyad and the inner life of the individual over social relationships, excludes the influence—both external and internal—of the social group and the social world. In contrast, group analysts tend to emphasize the world of shared social experience over that of individual internal experience. What is needed is a blending of the two, and this book sets out to accomplish just that.

In a way, this division goes back to Freud's abandonment of the seduction theory—which for all its shortcomings was a theory of social influence on individual development—in favor of seeing mind as constructing experience from inside itself to meet its inner needs. The theory of social influence, beginning with Fairbairn's view of the child as internalizing the mother as an internal object, received a more complete statement first from Pichon-Rivière, who saw the individual as constituted by others in family and social groups from the beginning. Drawing on the work of Kurt Lewin, Pichon-Rivière saw the field (“el campo”) as like a soccer pitch, with players in constant play and interaction, giving and taking meaning within that interaction.

The Ethical Seduction of the Analytic Situation: The Feminine-Maternal Origins of Responsibility for the Other. By Viviane Chetrit-Vatine. Translated by Andrew Weller. London: Karnac Books, 2014, xxii + 215 pp., $39.95 paperback.

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

11 Mountain Laurel Lane, Apt. 109
Rhinebeck, NY 12572
E-mail: mcavell@earthlink.com

Marcia Cavell   Associate Professor Emerita, Purchase College, State University of New York.

In this interesting book, Viviane Chetrit-Vatine takes up the themes of the origins of morality, responsibility, and the analyst's ethical obligations to the patient. Her principal thesis is that the mother's responsibility for the child—a relationship she will explore at length—offers a model for the analyst/patient relationship.

This strikes me as a deep insight. But, reader, be forewarned: the book is full of abstruse quasi-philosophical reflections that are not easy to follow. In some instances, for example her musings on gender, I just gave up. And her discussions of the French psychoanalytic tradition, which is her primary influence, make little effort to translate its idiosyncratic vocabulary into a language American analysts might more readily understand. A notable exception is her discussion of Emmanuel Levinas, whose writing is both powerful and relatively transparent, and with whom she begins.

Levinas claims that ethics is defined by the relationship of responsibility for another person. Further, the ought and the is, so to speak, are born together in the same fundamental situation, that of the infant's total dependence on the mother who is responsible for the infant. The is is the infant, the ought is the mother's relationship to the infant. As the theologian Hans Jonas—who influenced Levinas—puts it, “the plain being of a de facto infant immanently and evidently contains an ‘ought’ for others” (p. 19). There is thus a fundamental asymmetry built into the maternal situation: the mother owes the infant everything, the infant owes the mother nothing.

[[RunningHead]]

Working through the “Caesura”: A Clinical Illustration

Luca Caldironi

Luca Caldironi

Castello 1065
30122 Venezia
ITALY
E-mail: luca.caldironi@gmail.com

Luca Caldironi  

caesura, collusion, Bion, ludic space

More and more often analysts work with patients who must develop the capacity to participate in analysis. One such patient, H., conveyed the impression of being “bogged down.” He did not work or study, and at home with his family was prone to violent outbursts; his social relationships were centered around drug use. Concepts from Bion and Winnicott helped H.'s analyst take the risks necessary to create a therapeutic relationship with him. The patient has come to realize the worth of analytic thinking, and its potential to transform his life. Deviance with respect to technique, and collusion between the narcissistic areas of therapist and patient, pose the risk of dangerous slips in the direction of analytic “omnipotence.” Reclaiming the term collusion (from the Latin con-ludere, “to play together”), however, can allow analysts to remain faithful to Freud's ideas while maintaining a necessary flexibility vis-à-vis the patient. This ludic space can be expanded through Bion's ideas, which deconstruct a perspective through which we are able to observe “facts,” with particular reference to what occurs in the psychoanalytic situation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

해석 과정: “그저 말 일뿐”이라고 불리는 것의 힘

알란 스피박

알란 스피박

알란 스피박  

(The Interpretive Process: The Power of “Mere” Words): (Korean Translation)

안 나 O.는주치의인브로이어에게자신이받는"굴뚝-청소"의 치유적 효과를 이야기하는 와중에 "말하는 치료(talking cure)" 라는 용어를 새로 만들어 내었다. 그러나 효과가 지속되는 치 료는 그렇게 쉽게 일어나지 않는다고 세월이 지남에 따라 알려졌다. 해제 반응(abreaction) 의 방식에서 기인하는 그런 한계를 알게 되 자 프로이트는 실제로 더 섬세하고 복잡한 치료인 정신분석을 창 안하고자 하는 마음을 품었다. "말하는 치료"를 수정하면서, 치유 의 대부분은 의사가 환자의 말을 "번역(translation)”함에 따라서 이 루어진다고 생각도I 었다.

나는 치유((cure)와 번역 사이의 연관성 및 해석에 관해 말한 프 로이트 개념의 검토로부터 시작하려고 한다. 그리고 나서 프로이 트가 본래 언급하지는 않았지만, 변화를 기하급수적으로 확대할 수도 있는 힘을 가진, 분석가의 말에 의한 치료적인 측면을 탐구하 려 한다. 마지막으로 삽화 하나를 통해서 분석과정을 더 심도 있게 하고, 피분석가가 자신과의 관계 및 자신의 마음과의 관계를 변형 시키는데 있어서 해석이 가진 힘에 관하여 나는 예시하려 한다.1

꿈의 해식에서 프로이트는(1900) 프랑스의 심리학자인 모리 (Maury)를 인용한다: "충동이야 말로 그 자체로 말하고 있는 것이 고 또한 우리로 하여금 행동하게 하는 것이다”(P. 73 n. 1). 융에게 보낸 서신에서, 프로이트(1906)는 나중에 이 생각을 정신분석적 치 료를 위한 하나의 지침으로 발전시켰다: 전이는 무의식의 언어를 이해하고 번역하기 위해 필요한 충동을 제공한다; 이것이 결여되 면, 우리가 환자에게 번역을 제시해도 환자는 노력을 기울이지 않 거나 들으려고도 하지 않는다"(P. 11).

1내 입장에 관한 더 포괄적인 견해는, 스피바크 2011 을 보라.수련 및 지도 감독 분석가, 로스엔젤리스 정신분석 연구소 및 협호ᅵ. 2014 년, 7월 17일 출판을 위해 제출함.NP1

Il Sogno Necessario: Nuove teorie e techniche dell'interpretazione in psicoanalisi (The Necessary Dream: New Theories and Techniques of Interpretation in Psychoanalysis). By Giuseppe Civitarese. Milan: FrancoAngeli, 2013, 200 pp.I Sensi e l'inconscio (The Senses and the Unconscious). By Giuseppe Civitarese. Roma: Edizione Borla, 2014, 254 pp. 254.

Daria Colombo

Daria Colombo

35 East 85th Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: <dcolombo8@gmail.com>

Daria Colombo   Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Co-director, NYPSI Psychoanalytic Fellowship; Co-director, Payne Whitney Clinic Continuous Case Seminar.

Giuseppe Civitarese, with his prolific writing, has become increasingly known for his explication and promulgation of Bionian-inspired analytic field theory, which in his version includes a strong connection to early Freud and a profound interest in biological underpinnings and neurological endowments. Readers still unfamiliar with Civitarese might find both of these works rather an intense introduction, since they are built upon previous texts as Civitarese returns, in a more detailed manner, to many of the topics and methodologies dear to him. These are many and varied, with a particular use of his beloved cinema. Both works deepen his explorations of the function of dreaming in mental life and psychoanalytic practice, and while much will be familiar to his readers, in both these recent works Civitarese brings together most fully his radically different understanding of the nature and function of dreaming, an understanding that integrates Freud's initial insights with the neurobiology of representation and language and Bionian ideas about mentation. Civitarese is creating, in his extensive body of work, a virtual salon of sorts—and Civitarese loves the idea of virtual reality—in which the history of psychoanalysis, the impact of the intersubjective turn, the semiotics of representation, and the mind-body connection are in promiscuous interplay with one another and with a wealth of images from both high art and popular culture. The vitality and buzz of this overstuffed salon should not obscure the immense ambition of Civitarese's work, which is underscored by these two most recent works, especially when considered together. For Civitarese aims, to mixed but intriguing and certainly valiant effect, at no less than picking up where Freud left off in the abandoned Project for a Scientific Psychology.

This remarkable book is a transcript of a lecture series given by Silvia Bleichmar between April and November of 2004 at the Armenian Society, and in earlier years under the auspices of the Dr. Ricardo Gutiérrez Children's Hospital, both in Buenos Aires. Apart from the reader, Bleichmar's audience are her erudite pupils, ever present in the text with their “interventions,” replete with clinical case material and cultural references past and present. The reader quickly forms an image of Bleichmar as a master teacher whose range in Freudian theory is vast and whose energy and enthusiasm for demonstrating the relevance of even the most archaic of Freudian concepts applied directly to current issues is near inexhaustible. On these pages she comes across as witty, brilliant, and persistent. And this is important, because Bleichmar's goal here is to update Freudian psychoanalysis, in particular his sexual theory, so that it continues to be a viable practice and discourse.

Bleichmar, who died in 2007, was a world-renowned child psychoanalyst, popular in Latin America and Europe, and well known in her home city of Buenos Aires as a public intellectual and frequent contributor to newspapers, magazines, and other media. The course that constitutes this book represents one of eleven years of seminars that averaged around three hundred attendees, and from which all proceeds were donated to local mental health services. She was a Freudian, influenced to some extent by Klein and Winnicott, whose studies with Laplanche during her doctorate in psychoanalysis opened her perspective to his work. Earlier, before her exile from Argentina, she was influenced by Lacan but came to be critical of his structuralist model, which she viewed as limiting authentically transformative clinical work.

1065

[[RunningHead]]

Micro-trauma: A Psychoanalytic Understanding of Cumulative Psychic Injury. By Margaret Crastnopol. New York: Routledge, 2015, xvi + 252 pp., $199.95 hardcover, $49.95 paperback.

Carol Levy

Carol Levy

675 Seminole Avenue
Atlanta, GA 30307
E-mail: clevy@emory.edu

Carol Levy   Faculty, Emory Psychoanalytic Institute; adjunct faculty, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Emory University.

Micro-trauma: A Psychoanalytic Understanding of Cumulative Psychic Injury, the twenty-fifth volume in the Psychoanalysis in a New Key book

1266

series edited by Donnel Stern, clearly achieves Stern's goal of offering the analytic community something “that nudges the reader's grasp of clinical theory and practice in an unexpected direction.” The subject matter is the concept of micro-trauma, Crastnopol's original and seemingly oxymoronic term that describes the effects of subtle, relationally based injurious events that have “lesser degrees of insult and injury” than “capital T” trauma and that can chip away slowly at one's sense of self and well-being, often with the additional effect of undermining future relationships in their aftermath (p. 6). The author's conceptual framework of micro-trauma veers in a direction away from trauma as an acute event that overwhelms the individual's adaptive capacity. Instead, she posits that micro-trauma, though challenging rather than overwhelming, still requires adaptive and defensive psychological maneuvers to manage its effects. The author enjoins her readers to be open to her new terminology, preemptively countering the argument that applying the word trauma to anything other than “capital T” situations trivializes the concept. She offers some commonalities, proposing that micro-trauma, “like real-world trauma … reflects events in the external world and is not merely a private experience involving unconscious impulses and fantasies” (p. 6). Additionally, she notes that micro-traumas, like “capital T” traumas, are “real occurrences … that are experienced and absorbed to shape the intrapsychic world in particular ways that have lasting negative consequences …” (p. 6).

Crastnopol's book is clinical but provides ample theoretical inquiry and discussion on the genesis and psychological outcome of micro-trauma in nonclinical settings. Most significant are two important contributions she brings to contemporary psychoanalysis. First, she develops a new nomenclature, both the term micro-trauma and her conceptualization of seven types of micro-traumatic relating (“idiosyncratically” constructed, by Crastnopol's own admission), derived from clinical and personal experiences and informed by the psychoanalytic models that have most influenced her clinical thinking: interpersonal, relational, and object relations. Her nomenclature is clever, descriptively apt, and evocative, making it easy to envisage the damaging relational and intrapsychic maneuvers in each troublesome mode of relating. All of Crastnopol's relational patterns set the stage for psychic injury.

[[RunningHead]]

The Burdens of Culture and Era

An Emotional State: The Politics of Emotion in Postwar West German Culture. By Anna M. Parkinson. Ann Arbor: University of Michigan Press, 2015, viii + 251 pp., $70.00 hardcover, $30.00 paperback.

Herbert Arnold

Herbert Arnold

1 Edwards Road
Portland, CT 06480
E-mail: haaarnold@wesleyan.edu

Herbert Arnold   Professor emeritus of German Studies and Letters, Wesleyan University.

This interesting study proposes to investigate “the dialogue between the history of emotions and affect theory” (p. vi) in the context of West German history during the years between the end of World War II and the beginning of the Wirtschaftswunder, or “economic miracle,” of the fifties and early sixties. Or, as the author indicates in a footnote, “the modest objective of this book is to study the politics of emotion in postwar West German culture” (p. 173, n. 26). Ideally, such an undertaking would involve a theory of emotions applicable to a large population, which would then be applied to or measured against the historical record. That immense project is, quite understandably, not undertaken here. What we have instead is a detailed reading of texts generated by three primary sources working in very different areas of text production: an existentialist philosopher, Karl Jaspers; a nihilistic Prussian Junker with a violent pro-Nazi past, Ernst von Salomon; and two iconic postwar social scientists, Margarete and Alexander Mitscherlich. The writings of these four authors stand in, pars pro toto, for the vast literature generated during those years in a variety of fields. Newspapers and journals, literary productions from poetry to novels, personal accounts, and documentary collections on de-Nazification, of course, would also yield significant insights into the state of emotions in West Germany at that time, and on a sociological level different from that of the three academics and one political activist studied here. This is not a criticism of the book or its author but merely a delineation of the parameters within which this study operates.

1289

1435 Lexington Avenue

Henry Lothane   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai.

In the history of psychoanalysis, Freud's alleged sexual relationship with his sister-in-law Minna Bernays and Jung's alleged affair with Sabina Spielrein have become sensations, driving debates among defenders and doubters and spawning a considerable literature. Barry G. Gale's two books about Freud and Minna are a recent contribution to this genre.

Belle Vue, the earlier and lesser of the books, is called “a novel” on the dust jacket, but reads more like the script of a play. The author, a Ph.D. in history and philosophy of science from the University of Chicago, studied for two years with Bruno Bettelheim, who was on the faculty there. The book consists of rambling imaginary conversations, all taking place on July 24, 1895, between historical and invented dramatis personae, including Minna, her mother Emmeline (who “wore a pair of Cartier garnet earrings with bracelet to match” [p. 7]), and Freud and his wife Martha. Gale has Minna saying to Freud: “Sigmund, the time we spent together at the Schweizerhaus, it was the greatest time of my life” (p. 45), even though that event occurred in 1898, three years after this fictional conversation (see Lothane 2015).

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Acknowledging the “Analyst as Person”: A Developmental Achievement

Susan Kattlove

Susan Kattlove

104 Larch Road
Cambridge, MA 02138-3317
E-mail: skattlove@comcast.net

Susan Kattlove  

Ientered psychoanalytic training as I imagine many people do: not fully aware of my motivations, a little frightened and a little excited by the prospect of being an analyst, but clear about my idealization of theory and “proper” technique. One of my supervisors had told me as a resident that I didn't know much theory, but that my intuition, what he called “shooting from the hip,” seemed to be working pretty well. Psychoanalytic training was supposed to remedy that. Theory in hand, or head, I would be able to understand what my patients were telling me just the way I would interpret lab tests—objectively and free from the influence of my own personhood. I would provide my patients an experience that was solely about them, so they could work through their conflicts and traumas, transferences and projections, without me muddying the field. This, I thought, was the right, the ethical, thing to do. Coming of age psychoanalytically as intersubjective theory was becoming mainstream, I soon faced challenges to my ideal of analytic objectivity.

The question I would like to investigate is the ethical implications of acknowledging the analyst's personhood. Perhaps the more relevant way of phrasing this is the ethical implications of disavowing our personhood. Because that, I think, is the point, and it is an issue that I, and our field, has had trouble coming to terms with. Each of us is a person with our own particular unconscious conflicts, motivations, and ways of relating. And these aspects of ourselves, what Jane Kite (2008) might call the character that we analyze from within, are with us in every clinical encounter.

Faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Lecturer in Psychiatry (part-time), Harvard Medical School.

Submitted for publication September 3, 2016.

responseSet.authorMast: António Pazo Pires,  João Gonçalves,  Vânia Sá,  Andrea Silva and  Rolf Sandell
responseSet.authorCitation: Pires, A. P., Gonçalves, J., Sá, V., Silva, A. & Sandell, R.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Assessment of Change in Psychoanalysis: Another Way of Using the Change after Psychotherapy Scales

António Pires , João Gonçalves , Vânia Sá , Andrea Silva and Rolf Sandell

Rolf Sandell

34 Rua Jardim do Tabaco
Lisboa 1149-041
PORTUGAL
E-mail: apires@ispa.pt

Rolf Sandell  

psychic change, psychoanalysis, CHAP

A systematic method is presented whereby material from a full course of psychoanalytic treatment is analyzed to assess changes and identify patterns of change. Through an analysis of session notes, changes were assessed using the CHange After Psychotherapy scales (CHAP; Sandell 1987a), which evaluate changes in five rating variables (symptoms, adaptive capacity, insight, basic conflicts, and extratherapeutic factors). Change incidents were identified in nearly every session. Early in the analysis, relatively more change incidents related to insight were found than were found for the other types of change. By contrast, in the third year and part of the fourth year, relatively more change incidents related to basic conflicts and adaptive capacity were found. While changes related to symptoms occurred throughout the course of treatment, such changes were never more frequent than other types of change. A content analysis of the change incidents allowed a determination of when in the treatment the patient's main conflicts (identified clinically) were overcome. A crossing of quantitative data with clinical and qualitative data allowed a better understanding of the patterns of change.

responseSet.title: Response to Commentaries
responseSet.authorMast: Otto F. Kernberg and  Robert Michels
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Commentaries

Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels

Robert Michels

Robert Michels  

We were pleased and somewhat surprised by the broad agreement expressed by our discussants, and by the fact that most of their disagreements relate to the feasibility and implementation of our suggestions, not to our principles or basic positions. We will first discuss several general issues and then address specific comments by each of the discussants.

General Issues

Training Analyses

Everyone believes that the training analysis system has outlived its usefulness. Some, like Otto Kernberg, see it as a cause of major problems, and want to abolish it. Others, like Robert Michels, see it as a result of major problems and expect it to wither away as those problems are addressed. Practically, this makes little difference—we all envision a process of change leading to a situation in which all qualified analysts are training analysts. The current multiclass system of analysts, with all its attendant problems, will disappear.

Standards, Objectivity, Transparency, and Certification

For every analyst to be a training analyst, we must have criteria to ascertain that every analyst is a good enough analyst. In the past, the most serious evaluation of analysts has been embedded in the process of assessing potential training analysts. In the new system serious evaluation will occur at an earlier stage in an analyst's progression—at graduation and certification. Several commentators note that developing such a system would be difficult and expensive, and that the result would never be perfect. We thoroughly agree, but the alternative is to give up our claim of being a profession deserving of public support. Yes, it will be difficult, expensive, and forever incomplete, but it must be done and we can do it better than anyone else.

[[RunningHead]]

The Good Fight Continues

Robert Pyles

Robert Pyles

367 Worcester Street
Wellesley, MA 02481
E-mail: rpylesmd@comcast.net

Robert Pyles  

At the end of my first term as APsaA president, on November 25, 2000, in the grand ballroom of the Waldorf Astoria, I delivered a plenary address titled “The Good Fight: Psychoanalysis in the Age of Managed Care” (Pyles 2003). At that time, I expressed the hope that we would focus our efforts on two major organizational objectives. Coming now to the end of my second term, I have the opportunity to reflect on how we have done.

First, I called upon our association to embrace—fully and forcefully—the tasks of engagement with the world around us: to take on the major political and social threats to our profession and to our patients, to come out from behind our couches and form a network of analysts interacting with the public, with prospective patients, with the media, and with lawmakers at every relevant level of government.

Second, I urged us to turn our gaze inward as well as outward, in order to revisit and reconsider our entrenched habits of governance and education, as well as our attitudes toward analysts outside our association—analysts with whom we share our challenges, our fortunes, and a common fate. With leaner models of governance and a new spirit of collectivism, I hoped, we could confront our changing world with a growing sense of openness to opportunities for growth, a spirit of experimentation, and a commitment to collaboration among us all.

Looking at ourselves today, I am immensely proud of the remarkable advances we have made—and continue to make—regarding our engagement with the world around us. I am enormously proud of our extensive and effective lobbying efforts on behalf of our profession, of our vigorous advocacy with regard to critical social issues, and am deeply encouraged to see the considerable advances we have made in reaching out to the media.

Presidential address, American Psychoanalytic Association, Chicago, June 8, 2014.

161

responseSet.title: The Fundamental Ethical Ambiguity of the Analyst as Person
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.newSectionName: Ethical Implications of the Analyst as Person
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fundamental Ethical Ambiguity of the Analyst as Person

Jane Kite

Jane Kite

209 Pearl Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: jvkite@gmail.com

Jane Kite  

Psychoanalysis, from its beginnings in Studies on Hysteria, has been defined by its preoccupation with what is not known. Our central premise is that the unknown causes the illnesses that we treat; the idea defines both our theory and our praxis.

—Jay Greenberg (2016, p. 269)

That psychoanalysis is deep down a practice founded on ethics may seem obvious, even trivial, and, for that reason, be seriously underestimated.

— Dominique Scarfone (in press)

The analyst you become is you and you alone.

—Wilfred Bion (1975, p. 15)

Psychoanalysis as a discipline is based on what is not known. It has been argued in fact that Freud's singular contribution to the field of ethics is his straightforward acknowledgment of the unconscious (unknown) determinants of human behavior (Wallwork 1991). If ethics has been implicit in psychoanalysis from its beginnings in what is not known, it also poses an infernal question: How do we as analysts take (ethical) responsibility for aspects of our own behavior and motivations that inevitably remain unknown to us?

It is paradoxical in this context that almost everything we read in psychoanalysis is written as if we know (understand) what we're doing as analysts. This is partially true, of course, but also in very significant ways untrue. None of us know who we are as people in any fundamental sense, and many of us become psychoanalysts in an effort to find out.

Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute. Submitted for publication October 7, 2016.

[[RunningHead]]

The Ethical Foundation of Analytic Action

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

2960A Piedmont Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94705
E-mail: mdwmd@comcast.net

Mitchell Wilson  

We shall give up the idea that there are special classes of processes that prepare or propel mental activity, that is to say, classes that are qualitatively different from the mental activity they prepare or propel; for now everything is an action.

—Roy Schafer (1976, p. 13)

An ethics essentially consists in a judgment of our action, with the proviso that [the judgment] is only significant if the action implied by it also contains within it, or is supposed to contain, a judgment, even if it is only implicit. The presence of judgment on both sides is essential to the structure.

—Jacques Lacan (1959-1960, p. 311)

After Schafer and Lacan, Renik (1993a,b) and Friedman (1993, 2007), and, more or less, the entire relational tradition (Harris 2011), it is by now a commonplace to say that psychoanalysts are interested in their own activity—that is, our activity, what we are doing with our patients, what we, as analysts, say, feel, and imagine. Analysts have grasped the ways in which their internal worlds shape, illuminate, or constrain engagement with, and perhaps the very emergence of, the patient's subjective experience. Analysands talk in the hope we will receive their message generatively, openly, all the while taking the risk that we might misrecognize, not hear, and fail to witness what they want us to recognize, hear, and witness. This risk involves, as Shulman (2016) says, “a bid for utmost intimacy” (p. 706); the emotional stakes are high. In this context, it is no wonder we care deeply about how we act and why we act as we do.

Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

Submitted for publication September 20, 2016.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Finding Control Cases and Maintaining Immersion: Challenges and Opportunities

Lena Ehrlich

Lena Ehrlich

555 East William Street, Suite 22C
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
E-mail: lenaehrlich@comcast.net

Lena Ehrlich  

analytic identity, candidates, control cases, countertransference, finding control cases, immersion, initiating analysis, initial consultation, psychoanalytic training, transference, resistance

Given that surveys, as well as frequent observations by institute faculty, indicate that many candidates have difficulty finding control cases and maintaining immersion and that many graduate analysts face similar challenges, it would seem that psychoanalytic training does not prepare candidates adequately for finding patients and practicing analysis while in training and, for many, after they have graduated. Although external challenges are formidable, it is by identifying and making use of internal challenges to finding cases that candidates can develop an analytic mind: the identity, approach, and skills necessary not only to graduate but to have the choice to practice clinical psychoanalysis post-graduation. Some of the internal challenges and their manifestations in different phases of initiating analysis (referrals, initial consultation, recommendation) are discussed and two detailed examples are offered to illustrate the productive use of candidates' countertransferences in finding cases and maintaining immersion. Finally, recommendations for institutional solutions are provided.

[[RunningHead]]

Thoughts on the Present and Future of Psychoanalytic Education

Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels

Robert Michels

21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605
E-mail: <okernberg@med.cornell.edu>

450 East 63rd Street
New York NY 10065-7928
E-mail: <rmichels@med.cornell.edu>

Robert Michels  

This paper originated in a series of dialogues between the authors over a period of approximately one year, focused on present problems (and possible solutions) in psychoanalytic education, internationally but particularly in the U.S. Both authors have been involved in psychoanalytic education and governance over many years and share a concern with where psychoanalysis presently stands and where it is going. They share the experience of being part of what today is a significant minority of psychoanalysts involved in academic pursuits, thus being situated at the boundary between psychoanalysis and university-based psychiatry as professions. Having been involved in the leadership of both psychoanalytic and psychiatric organizations, they share an interest in organizational theory, an additional joint interest influencing their approach to institutional aspects of psychoanalysis. Despite these commonalities, however, they have been identified as having differently shaded positions and views within psychoanalytic politics: Robert Michels, as relatively conservative regarding controversial issues in psychoanalytic institutional functioning and governance, and Otto Kernberg as inclined toward rapid change regarding these issues (Auchincloss and Michels 2003; Kernberg 2014). This difference determined a dynamic of particular interest in their dialogue: the extent to which mutual respect yet differing viewpoints might issue in an analysis and joint recommendations that might be of interest to the field. In what follows, their achievement in that direction is spelled out in some detail.

Psychoanalysis has played a significant role in the development of twentieth-century Western culture, an influence that extends to the present. It is also a major scientific enterprise dealing with fundamental

Otto F. Kermberg, Director, Personality Disorders Institute, New York-Presbyterian Hospital, Westchester Division; Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research. Robert Michels, Walsh McDermott University Professor of Medicine and Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Critique of Regression: The Person, the Field, the Lifespan

Gregory Rizzolo

Gregory Rizzolo

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1450
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: greg.rizzolo@gmail.com

Gregory Rizzolo  

regression, unconscious, development, lifespan, adult development, relational theory, Erikson, field, person

The term regression refers to the idea that a person can return to earlier phases of mental development and the primitive modes of functioning associated with them. A core concept in both conflict and deficit models of development, the idea has nonetheless come under increasing scrutiny from critics who argue that it misleads us into a genetic fallacy whereby we reduce the issues of adolescent and adult development to their childhood precursors. Inderbitzen and Levy (2000) suggest that we focus on transformations, or shifts, in mental organization, instead of on regressions. But discarding the concept of regression has theoretical implications: to adopt instead a focus on shifts in mental organization we must (1) consider our object of study to be the meaning-making person, not isolated instincts or needs; (2) understand conscious and unconscious mental life to be embedded in the here-and-now relational field; and (3) adopt a lifespan model of development. The aim here is to outline a theoretical framework in which we can more fully explore the possibility of discarding “regression” in favor of a focus on transformations in the developmental present.

Dorothy Holmes

Dorothy Holmes

10 Pinckney Colony Road
Suite 315, Bldg. 300
Bluffton, SC 29909
E-mail: <crescent@gwu.edu>

Dorothy Holmes  

As you begin to listen to this plenary presentation, what about me do you take into account—me as scholar, as practicing analyst, as retired academic, as older, as female, as married, as Black? As I ponder these questions, I feel a mix of eager anticipation, wariness, lively intellectual curiosity, and a desire to convince you that what I have to say is important, not only to me, but for you, and for psychoanalysis. I hope to convince you that how our society treats and mistreats its nondominant members is deeply and importantly represented in how all of us live—in our daily lives, in our psyches, and in the psychoanalytic treatments we provide.

Intrapsychic Manifestations and Effects of Culture, History, Neurosis, and Character

I, like many, have multiple cultural identities. A very important one for me, in terms of both my deepest satisfaction and my greatest challenge, is my identity as Black. The fact that I am on this stage talking to you reflects both—satisfaction and challenge.

Professor and Program Director Emerita, Center for Professional Psychology, The George Washington University; Teaching, Training, and Supervising Psychoanalyst Emerita, Baltimore Washington Center for Psychoanalysis.

Plenary Address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 15, 2016.

The Insane Look of the Bewildered Half-Broken Animal

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place
New York, NY 11201
E-mail: donaldmoss@mindspring.com

Donald Moss  

To have attacks … that are of a sexual nature perpetrated by men ostensibly of Muslim origin is symbolically devastating for a public commitment to asylum.

—Alexander Betts, director of the Refugee Studies Center at Oxford University (New York Times, January 13, 2016)

[German Chancellor Angela] Merkel reiterated calls that “we must continually re-examine whether we really have done everything, as far as expulsion orders and actual expulsions from Germany are concerned.”

—Alison Smalejan (New York Times, January 8, 2016)

We are alive at a very dark time. We have good reason to doubt the inevitability of historical progress, of civilizations holding together, of thought maintaining its relevance. We can sense the reemergence of the Crusader mentality, the increased and terrifying presence of both organized and lone-wolf predators. Ours is a time of desperate people, desperate cities, and a ravaged planet, of madmen and of dragons. Yet somehow, it might also be a time for psychoanalysis. But how? How can we locate ourselves here and now? How can we legitimate our claim to still belong?

Plenary address, American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 15, 2016.

In Memory of Johanna Bodenstad and Ornette Coleman.

responseSet.title: Masochism and Its Rhythm

[[RunningHead]]

Masochism and Its Rhythm

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1
27100 Pavia
ITALY
E-mail: gcivitarese@gmail.com

Giuseppe Civitarese  

beating fantasies, masochism, fantasy in the body, negative pleasure, trauma, rhythm

Over five years, from 1919 to 1924, Freud dealt with masochism in three texts written in close proximity: “A Child Is Being Beaten,” Beyond the Pleasure Principle, and “The Economic Problem of Masochism.” Initially Freud explains masochism as incestuous fixation on the father and regression to pregenital, sadistic ways of loving. Subsequently he considers it primarily as subservient to the death drive. This paper starts from an idea present in two of the three texts, but not developed by Freud, in which he refers to the role that the “qualitative” element of rhythm could play in the occurrence of pleasure in masochism. By means of this element traumatic aspects of the primary relationship with the object could be stored as fantasies in the body. In any staged masochistic fantasies of being beaten or in masochistic perversion, the pleasure of pain would lie in the attempt to “dream” the trauma not only in the imagination but also, “aesthetically,” in the body.

Obsessionality: Modulating the Encounter with Emotional Truth and the Aesthetic Object

Avner Bergstein

Avner Bergstein

14 Nahum Street
Ramat Gan 5223344
ISRAEL
E-mail: avnerbergstein@gmail.com

Avner Bergstein  

aesthetic conflict, autistic states, Bion, Meltzer, obsessionality, primitive mental states, unrepressed unconscious

Experiences with autistic and primitive mental states have significant implications for our understanding of obsessionality. Consequently, obsessionality is seen as an attempt at a massive simplification of experience, in order to deal with the pain inherent in the encounter with intense emotional experience and with the separateness of an enigmatic object that eludes one's omnipotent control. Moreover, early loss and a precocious awareness of separateness often play roles in the withdrawal to obsessional thinking and verbosity, and to an illusion of omnipotent control of the object. Interpretations focusing on conflicting desires, or linking repressed and displaced parts of the personality with the defenses against them, do not reach these patients in a way that facilitates psychic change. An alternative approach, it is suggested, is to work at primitive, nonsymbolic levels of mental functioning, where experience cannot be verbally communicated and dynamically interpreted, but must first be lived in the here and now of the analysis. This is illustrated through the analysis of a person trying to cope with the experience of early loss by deadening emotion and finding shelter in obsessionality.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fear of Breakdown in the Psychoanalytic Group: Commentary on Dimen

Charles Levin

Charles Levin

4999 Ste-Catherine West
Westmount, Quebec H3Z IT3
CANADA
E-mail: charleslevin@videotron.ca

Charles Levin  

An important indication of cultural evolution in psychoanalysis has been our increasing acknowledgment that we not only love but also passionately hate psychoanalysis, even and especially when it is our discipline of choice. We have ideas about what good psychoanalysis ought to be, and we want to embody that goodness for our patients, students, and others. At the same time, we admit to feelings of hatred toward this good thing precisely because it demands so much of us.

In Muriel's Dimen's earlier work on boundary violations, “Lapsus Linguae, or a Slip of the Tongue?” (2011), she emphasized the need for the analyst to negotiate love and hate in the best interests of the patient, just as a parent must learn to resonate with, and simultaneously contain, the joy and pain of unrequited libidinal love, both the child's for the parent, and the parent's for the child (pp. 56-58). In offering this analogy, Dimen has enriched our concept of what is involved in a sexual boundary violation. Apart from the issue of overt sexual relations, she insists on the clinical and ethical importance of the analyst's nonappropriative stance: this involves a deep respect for the personal autonomy latent in the patient's struggle to express and somehow realize newly discovered desires and archaic, possibly unanswerable longings. When the analyst is psychically unable to contain the arousal of these energies in the mutual transference field, or to make good reparative use of their inevitable enactment, then the analytic opportunity to integrate desire as one's own may be lost to the patient (pp. 58-62). Underlying every sexual boundary violation lies a subtler and sometimes more insidious “narcissistic boundary violation.

Director, Canadian Institute of Psychoanalysis (Quebec English Branch); Editor, Canadian Journal of Psychoanalysis / Revue canadienne de psychanalyse.
