responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rotten Apples and Ambivalence: Sexual Boundary Violations through a Psychocultural Lens

Muriel Dimen

Muriel Dimen

Muriel Dimen  

Sexual boundary violations ferry analysts and patients from thought to action, from the symbolic toward the materiality of life. They bring the outside in. Yes, they are enactments of fantasy. At the same time, though, sexual transgressions are workings of power and vehicles of culture. They evoke the social context for psychoanalysis, its rules, politics, and disciplinary power, its ethics committees, the legal systems that license and regulate training and clinicians, define and punish breaches of sexual boundaries by the licensed. Sexual boundary violations make trouble for any group. And they are a problem of the group too.

Most psychoanalytic thinking about this problem tends to take an inside view. I am going to do so too, but I am also going to view it from the outside in. Thinking as an anthropologist, I consider psychoanalysis an institution that, like any other social unit, has a structure, history, rules, and beliefs. Since, I am, of course, an insider, I can in no way claim objectivity. Nor would I want to. But my frame in this paper is different: a multiperspectival approach to this enigma, a position I am naturally

Muriel Dimen died on February 14, 2016, as this paper was being prepared for publication. Dimen, who originally trained as an anthropologist, qualified as a psychoanalyst in 1983 after having completed the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. Throughout her long and distinguished career, she retained a keen interest in the interplay between the intrapsychic, the intersubjective, the cultural, and the political. A faculty member, supervisor, and training analyst at the NYU Postdoctoral Program and several other analytic institutes, she was editor of the interdisciplinary journal Studies in Gender and Sexuality, an associate editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, a founding board member of the International Association of Relational Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, and the author of numerous books and scholarly articles on sexuality, gender, and power.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Can Babies Remember Trauma? Symbolic Forms of Representation in Traumatized Infants

Susan Coates

Susan Coates

205 West 89th Street
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: swcoates@gmail.com

Susan Coates  

infant memory, infant trauma, somatic memory, attachment, trauma

Three important areas of current inquiry concerning early trauma—the respective roles of reality and fantasy, age-related capacity for the symbolic representation of trauma, and attachment status—are approached through clinical case reports of three children seen initially at very early ages. The findings are relevant to the issue of whether preverbal infants can experience traumatic events that later are available to interpretation. The focus is for the most part on event traumas—single harrowing, life-threatening experiences—occurring at quite early ages. Three main points are emphasized. First, toddlers and infants (including neonates) can experience intense pain and show symptoms of traumatization. They are capable of experiencing an event as harrowing and life-threatening. Second, these events are capable of being memorialized or symbolically represented, that is, stored in memory in a way that can affect later behavior and learning. Third, how that traumatization resolves itself, or fails to, can be decisively affected by the functioning of the attachment system.

[[RunningHead]]

Illusions of a Future: Psychoanalysis and the Biopolitics of Desire. By Kate Schechter. Durham: Duke University Press, 2014, viii + 277 pp., $84.95 hardcover, $23.95 paperback.

David Carlson

David Carlson

255 Bradley Street
New Haven, CT 06510
E-mail: david.carlson@yale.edu

David Carlson   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Yale University School of Medicine; Training and Supervising Analyst, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Is it ethical to offer education in psychoanalysis? The inescapable implication to be drawn from a reading of Kate Schechter's Illusions of a Future is that it is not, at least in Chicago. She reports that psychoanalytic practice there has been dead since the 1970s, maintained for a while in a zombie-like state through the smoke-and-mirrors administrative and financial machinations of George Pollock. The recent Chicago Institute graduates she interviews have few or no patients in analysis, though each clings to an image of analysis—in each case a different image. Such allegiance to an absent entity she characterizes as “fetishization,” and the analysts she presents do come across as pathetic in their devotion to, and invocation of, an illusion.

Schechter's message is somewhat obscured by her ambitions, when setting out on this book, to provide a braided account of “several modes of analysis at once: a textual analysis of the place of crisis in professional discourse; a structural account of psychoanalysis's difficult history of institutional reproduction in the United States; an ethnographic and historical reading of a group of psychoanalysts’ attempts to define their practices in positive terms; and a political-philosophical analysis of the ‘resistances’ of psychoanalysis that make it so seemingly impossible for psychoanalysts to organize collectively to resist health-care industrialization” (pp. 5-6). If this seems an impossible burden for one volume, it is. Strands of thought begin, but then are never conclusively brought into contact with the subject matter, or else confusingly shift their meanings without warning.

245

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Offer

Humphrey Morris

Humphrey Morris

8 Irving Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138-3011
E-mail: humphreymorris@gmail.com

Humphrey Morris  

How does psychoanalysis begin, how does it come into being, what makes it start to happen as something new between this analysand and this analyst? This is the ethical question I will take up. Call it the ethics of the analyst's offer.

The offer is the opening into a foundational ethics for psychoanalysis. This ethics asks how the analytic situation is secured in the first place, how the frame is set. Foundational ethics underlies the operational ethics of psychoanalytic practice, something that is easier to talk about. Operational ethics is more experience-near. It takes up, moment by moment and instance by instance, all the gnarly questions of how practicing analysts must attempt to attend to the frame, attempt to maintain it, especially at the inevitable moments of disorientation we call enactments. Foundational ethics, compared to this, risks seeming cerebral. It starts from the seemingly abstract question of how what we call the analyst's stance makes possible psychoanalytic treatment in the first place. Lacan's 1959-1960 seminar is one of the major efforts at delineating a foundational psychoanalytic ethics. In 2012 the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association acknowledged the importance of Lacan's effort in an issue centered on an article by Lewis Kirshner. But long before that, in a plain-spoken style that couldn't be less Lacanian, Lawrence Friedman took on questions of foundational ethics in his trenchant 1994 American Psychoanalytic Association plenary address, “Ferrum, Ignis, and Medicina: Return to the Crucible” (1997). I'll start there.

Friedman wants to make us squirm—that's the word he uses (1997, p. 22)—as he asks us to think about what it is we are offering as analysts. Freud's effort to make great discoveries, Friedman writes, “had unexpectedly put in his hands a peculiar power—the power of psychological seduction” (p. 25). “I shouldn't have to—” Friedman continues, “but I've

Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

[[RunningHead]]

Is There a Usable Heidegger for Psychoanalysts?

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: <oldtemplegate@gmail.com>

Lawrence Friedman  

Heidegger, time, phenomenology, hermeneutics, abstraction, representation, social construction of reality, holism

If any sort of philosophical ‘confrontation’ … is going to be in any sense fruitful, the critic must accordingly decide to stand upon the ground Heidegger has selected. Whether he can remain thereon is a question that can only be decided through the exposition itself.

—Ernst Cassirer (in Gordon 2010, p. 272)

Our facing this difficulty that stems from language should keep us from hastily recasting the language of the thinking here attempted into the coin of a terminology, and from speaking right away about perdurance, instead of devoting all our efforts to thinking through what has been said. For what was said was said in a seminar. A seminar, as the word implies, is a place and an opportunity to sow a seed here and there, a seed of thinking which some time or other may bloom in its own way and bring forth fruit.

—Martin Heidegger (1957, pp. 73-74)

These days psychoanalysts are often asked to consider their work in terms of the influential and monumentally difficult philosopher Martin Heidegger. For reasons I present below, there is much room in Heidegger's sort of speculation for varying perspectives, even among specialists. Thus I have every reason to expect vigorous disagreement with the perspective I present here.

Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College; faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Education affiliated with the New York University School of Medicine.

Submitted for publication December 31, 2015.

[[RunningHead]]

Slouching towards Mortality: Thoughts on Time and Death

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

5225 Connecticut Avenue
Washington, DC 20015
E-mail: warrenpoland@gmail.com

Warren Poland  

And next year's words await another voice.

—T. S. Eliot, “Little Gidding”

For me, it felt in my life like the break of day. I was in my late twenties, married and with two young children, at long last moving out from life fully bound by outer structures: college, medical school, internship, residency, and the then required two years' uniformed service time. Eager to take full ownership of life, I was shocked when the doctor at my service discharge physical announced I should not resign from the service. “Why?” I demanded. Her words still echo: “Because your wife will need widow's benefits.”

That was one of three times doctors in different circumstances told me I likely had a fatal disease. Each of those times had a happy outcome, yet I know a time will come with a different ending. The ease with which now I reflect on transience is far from the cold terror that attends the imminent presence of death.

Death is a fact; its subjective impact varies with distance. I move a touch closer, from times that worked out well to one that did not. Standing at the bedside of a hospitalized friend on a day we both knew would be one of his last, I mentioned Prospero's line, “Every third thought will be my grave.” My friend fell momentarily silent, then smiled and said, “I like that—every third thought, not every second.”

Death's proximity concentrates one's mind. Desire to distance attention to the imminence of my own demise is apparent in the path I followed essaying this concept.

Former editor, JAPA Review of Books.

795

[[RunningHead]]

Will This Case Count? The Influence of Training on Treatment

Rachel Blakeman and Marianne Goldberger

Marianne Goldberger

240 East 76th Street (Ground Floor)
New York, NY 10021-2909
E-mail: rachelblakeman@gmail.com

Marianne Goldberger  

the couch, psychoanalytic training, candidates, training cases, face-to-face psychoanalysis

Mandated use of the couch—whether specifically stated or tacitly communicated by supervisors and colleagues—to fulfill requirements for graduation or certification is a significant disservice to candidates and their patients. In its training standards, it is argued, APsaA and its member institutes should state explicitly that a treatment can qualify as a psychoanalysis, regardless of whether the patient is using the couch, as long as the process is analytic and the candidate's thinking is demonstrably analytic. The mandate, however conveyed, that one must use the couch interferes with candidates' optimal analytic functioning, jeopardizing their patients' analyses. Data from infant observation, neuroscience, and facial expression studies—unavailable to earlier generations of analysts—support a more nuanced view of use of the couch. Each analysis is unique, and some analyses might well benefit from use of both the couch and the chair at different phases of treatment, but unless this is spelled out by ApsaA and its member institutes, candidates and junior analysts will be prevented from freely contemplating the clinical benefits or detriments of their use in specific cases.

responseSet.title: Me Here, You There—Now What? Commentary on Kite, Morris, Wilson, and Kattlove
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Me Here, You There—Now What? Commentary on Kite, Morris, Wilson, and Kattlove

Donald Moss

Donald Moss

80 University Place
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: donaldmoss@mindspring.com

Donald Moss  

Some years ago I was told of an anti-malaria initiative in which public health officials showed a video of the anopheles mosquito to a group of villagers, hoping to help them identify the mosquito as the malaria vector. Returning to the village months later, the officials discovered that their video had done no good. Villagers concluded that the mosquito they saw on the video was much, much larger than any they had ever seen in their midst and therefore that the video had no pertinence.

Though apparently laughable, this sequence seems to me to perfectly expose a fundamental problem associated with using an example—the video here, yes, but more generally, any illustration, staged, recorded, or recounted, no matter how verbatim, no matter how apparently fitting—as a means of educating. An example achieves its educative intention only if the earlier moment it represents matches the present-day moment toward which it has been forwarded—when that-there-then replicates this-here-now.

The video's failure to achieve its aim, then, might usefully be thought of as a successful example of examples' general tendency to fail. After all, the particulars of any exemplary image from the past will never perfectly correspond to the particulars required by its receiving present. The point of contact between the present-day object and its exemplary predecessor will always leave a gap. No matter the example, we are all, I think, in the position of the villagers—it never quite fits. Our pathogenic mosquito is not exactly like the one represented in the example.

We might all wish it were otherwise.

Faculty, Institute for Psychoanalytic Education affiliated with NYU School of Medicine.

1217

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Mode of Thought and its Application to the Non-Normative Analysis of Sexuality and Gender

Brian Kloppenberg

Brian Kloppenberg

1133 Broadway, Suite 1127
New York, NY 10010
E-mail: bakloppenberg@aol.com

Brian Kloppenberg  

non-normative, homosexuality, sexuality, gender, psychoanalysis

Freud's psychoanalytic mode of thought, especially as interpreted by William Grossman, functions as an inherently non-normative means of analyzing both the psychic life of the individual and various historical and cultural phenomena. Application of this mode of thought to particular characterizations of gender and sexuality makes possible an essential critique of trait psychology and static formulations of human development, as restricting both clinical practice and theory building. An exploration of the complexities of sexuality and gender in gay men shows how Freud's insistence on variability lends conceptual power at the intersection of classical psychoanalysis, with its problematic history, and the many clinical and theoretical manifestations of contemporary psychoanalysis. The non-normative psychoanalytic mode of thought developed by Freud must be integrated with more recent formulations about gender and sexuality in order to conduct a psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Don't Fence Me in: What is American about American Psychoanalysis?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Don't Fence Me in: What is American about American Psychoanalysis?

Gavin Mullen

Gavin Mullen

910 Skokie Boulevard
Northbrook, IL 60062-3727
E-mail: gsm617@aol.com

Gavin Mullen  

In this 2016 presidential election year for both the American Psychoanalytic Association and the United States, it seemed fitting to address a panel at the January APsA meetings in New York with this question: “What is American in American psychoanalysis?” Our effort to understand our ever evolving identity seems a distinctly American activity. Perhaps our need to understand our identity is heightened in election years, when we size up ourselves as Americans and tackle what it means to identify ourselves as such. It might be that a new American identity has emerged in this election cycle. Psychoanalysts seem to have a stake, too, in knowing who we are and where we are from, because as Americans we imagine that who we become is within our own making.

The panelists, Donald Moss, Glen Gabbard, and Bonnie Litowitz, together with Shelley Orgel as chair, responded to the panel title, “What Is American about American Psychoanalysis?” as both a statement and a question. The panelists were bold in their statements, defining the American character as a mix of cowboy and pragmatist. They also took a scholarly tack in defining the “American” nature of American psychoanalysis, by weaving together the early strands of American literary culture (Twain's Huck Finn), philosophy (Emersonian transcendentalism, the pragmatism of James, Dewey, and Peirce), and the observations made by Tocqueville on his 1831 visit to America, when he identified many aspects of our national character.

Panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, January 16, 2016. Panelists: Shelley Orgel (chair), Glen O. Gabbard, Bonnie E. Litowitz, Donald Moss.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Confronting the Legitimation Crisis: Introduction to Kernberg and Michels

Stephen Portuges

Stephen Portuges

Stephen Portuges  

Nor would I say that our institutes are of no value. They do fulfil their purpose: they turn out … a remarkable percentage of competent analysts; but… the training that is conducted in our professional schools distorts some of the most valuable features of psychoanalysis and hinders its development as a science and as a tool by means of which to change behavior.

—Siegfried Bernfeld (1952)

The history of attempts to improve the education of psychoanalysts schooled in IPA or APsaA institutes dates back much further than Bernfeld's remarks, but this excerpt of his assessment could have been written, as you will soon see, by the authors of the centerpiece of the special section on psychoanalytic education to follow. Among others who have diagnosed its major limitations, recognized its significant strengths, and offered new educational options, the late Robert Wallerstein's prolific contributions to this discussion (1972, 1978, 1991, 1993, 1998, 2007a,b, 2009, 2011, 2012; Wallerstein and Weinshel 1989; Wallerstein and Fonagy 1999) are noteworthy; were he to have lived, he would have been one of the continuing contributors to this debate.

JAPA welcomes the collaborative paper by Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels that presents their assessment of the current state of psychoanalytic education and offers a comprehensive proposal for enhancing its future.

467

Of Rats and Men: The Defensive Human Brain/Mind

Anxious: Using therain to Understand and Treat Fear and Anxiety. By Joseph LeDoux. New York: Viking, 2015, xii + 466 pp., $28.95 hardcover.

Leon Hoffman

Leon Hoffman

167 East 67th Street
New York, NY 10065
E-mail: <hoffman.leon@gmail.com>

Leon Hoffman   Director, Pacella Parent Child Center, New York Psychoanalytic Society; Training and Supervising Analyst, New York Psychoanalytic Institute.

Joseph LeDoux, Henry and Lucy Moses Professor of Science at NYU's Center for Neural Science, director of that university's Emotional Brain Institute and of the Nathan Kline Institute, and rhythm guitarist and vocalist of the Amygdaloids,1 a rock band, began in the 1970s to study the functioning of the nervous system of rats during conditioning and extinction experiments and its implications regarding the functioning of the human brain.2 With Anxious: Using the Brain to Understand and Treat Fear and Anxiety, he has produced a magnum opus that should be required reading for all psychotherapists and analysts.

The book consists of eleven chapters, each building on the preceding one. Especially valuable are the many figures and tables, which illustrate the book's central points and explain them in a way the reader can easily understand. In addition, discussions in later chapters include references to earlier discussions of the same topic. This is a book not to skim but to study in depth.

The first four chapters introduce a variety of basic science constructs and experiments, including the development of LeDoux's thinking and work, leading to an understanding of the brain processes underlying the emergence of conscious fearful and anxious feelings.

1The Amygdaloids released Anxious the CD concurrently with the book under review (http://www.amygdaloids.com/?page_id=3478).2I am grateful to Wilma Bucci and Timothy R. Rice for their careful reading and useful suggestions.627

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Empirically Based Suggested Insights into the Concept of False-Self Defense: Contributions from a Study on Normalization of Children with Disabilities

Adva Eichengreen , Dan Hoofien and Eytan Bachar

Eytan Bachar

Mount Scopus, Jerusalem
ISRAEL
E-mail: adva.eichengreen@gmail.com

Eytan Bachar  

The concept of the false self has been used widely in psychoanalytic theory and practice but seldom in empirical research. In this empirically based study, elevated features of false-self defense were hypothetically associated with risk factors attendant on processes of rehabilitation and integration of children with disabilities, processes that encourage adaptation of the child to the able-bodied environment. Self-report questionnaires and in-depth interviews were conducted with 88 deaf and hard-of-hearing students and a comparison group of 88 hearing counterparts. Results demonstrate that despite the important contribution of rehabilitation and integration to the well-being of these children, these efforts may put the child at risk of increased use of the false-self defense. The empirical findings suggest two general theoretical conclusions: (1) The Winnicottian concept of the environment, usually confined to the parent-child relationship, can be understood more broadly as including cultural, social, and rehabilitational variables that both influence the parent-child relationship and operate independently of it. (2) The monolithic conceptualization of the false self may be more accurately unpacked to reveal two distinct subtypes: the compliant and the split false self.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Levy and Finnegan

Jeffrey Stern

Jeffrey Stern

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1300
Chicago, IL 60603
E-mail: sternjeffrey@me.com

Jeffrey Stern  

In “Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird” (1917), Wallace Stevens makes the point that meaning is not simply something inherent in an object of contemplation but something that emerges in the intersubjective relation between the object and its beholder. There are many ways of looking at dreams and no doubt countless ways of looking at any individual dream. As the authors of our specimen dreams note, Freud (1900) originally considered dreams in terms of the fulfillment of infantile wishes. Later he came to see anxiety dreams as efforts to master traumatic experience by preparing the psyche for what once overwhelmed it.

Bonnie Litowitz asks how several of us think of dreams and use them in our clinical work, giving me the opportunity to try to put into words my way (or ways) of looking at such blackbirds. I have been much influenced by Fosshage's idea (1997) that dreams represent the psyche's creative efforts to organize and synthesize experience coherently, rather than being productions of a drive-driven mechanism for the expression of forbidden wishes. And I like Bollas's idea that the atmosphere of the dream represents in part the subject's unthought known (1987, p, 70), that is, the look and feel of his or her psychologically constitutive universe. Freud taught us to ask our patients to associate to the images of any dream with a view toward revealing the latent dream thoughts behind the dream's so-called manifest content. But in a recent paper Atwood (2015) analyzes the manifest content of two dreams and argues that the dreamer's associations are often misleading in “locating the [all-important] context of experience” to which the dream belongs.

Faculty, Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis.

71

[[RunningHead]]

Heather Macdonald's H is for Hawk

H is for Hawk. By Helen Macdonald. New York: Grove Press, 2014, 300 pp., $26.00 hardcover, $16.00 paperback.

Rebecca Chaplan

Rebecca Chaplan

40 West 77th Street, 1F
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: rc361@cumc.columbia.edu

Rebecca Chaplan   Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Columbia University; Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research.

His for Hawk covers a lot of territory. Helen Macdonald has won many well-deserved and prestigious prizes for her lyrical memoir about training a hawk and how it helped her through the complicated mourning of her father's death. It is a literate book whose true subject is the relationship between psychic reality and the external world. An important figure in her story is her childhood hero, the author T. H. White, who also wrote about raising a hawk. Macdonald refers to ideas developed by psychoanalytic writers including Freud, Winnicott, and Loewald, bringing theory to life for a general readership.

As a memoir, it is incomplete. She explores her close tie to her father in great detail, yet tells almost nothing about the quality of her relationships with her surviving mother and brother. We don't know much about how she is situated in her life, to what extent she is inhibited in using her talents, why she doesn't have a job or a romantic partner. She is eloquent about the violence of her grief, but is quiet about her own dynamics, and we are left to wonder how much she is communicating about herself when she tells us about the shame that compromised the life of T. H. White. Altogether these are minor omissions in a profoundly moving book that addresses what it means to be human and live in the natural world. Ultimately the work is a philosophical meditation about finding one's place in the world, as an individual and in a community, closer in genre to Walden than to Wild. Although rooted in her particular life story, by following the web of her associations Macdonald explores ideas about sadomasochism in relationships, the use of fantasy to make reality tolerable, and the potential for survival of a coherent self in the face of trauma and loss.

195

[[RunningHead]]

Christopher Bollas on the Psychoanalytic Treatment of Schizophrenia

When the Sun Bursts: The Enigma of Schizophrenia. By Christopher Bollas. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2015, xiv + 226 pp., $28.00 hardcover.

Steven Poser

Steven Poser

8 Patchin Place, #2F
New York, NY 10011
E-mail: stevenposer@earthlink.net

Steven Poser   Faculty, Training Analyst, and Supervisor, Center for Modern Psychoanalytic Studies, New York; member, psychotherapy conference, Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai Hospital.

Christopher Bollas, rightly described as “one of the most creative and inspiring writers in the history of psychoanalysis” (Jemstedt 2011, p. xii), recounts in this heartfelt and beautifully written book what he has learned from working with schizophrenic patients beginning in the 1960s and continuing over five decades of clinical experience. The book, which he describes as a companion piece to the earlier Catch Them before They Fall: The Psychoanalysis of Breakdown (Bollas 2013), takes the form of a chronological memoir of his entire career as a psychotherapist, beginning as a twenty-three-year-old graduate in history from the University of California working with autistic and schizophrenic children at the East Bay Activity Center in Oakland; through his introduction to being a psychotherapist while a graduate student in English literature at the University of Buffalo; his training in psychoanalysis at the London Institute for Psychoanalysis and the Tavistock Clinic in the mid-seventies; his twenty years of private practice in London, during which he treated mainly severely disturbed psychotic patients; his work at the Austen Riggs Center in the mid-eighties; and on into the present. The book closes with a portrait of his work with a patient living on a remote island in a Norwegian fjord, with whom he spoke five days a week on the telephone from his farmstead in Pekin, North Dakota, where he spends half the year. Over the course of these fifty years, Bollas has written over a dozen books, which in their cumulative effect amount to a uniquely original re-visioning of the psychoanalytic enterprise from the ground up.

407

[[RunningHead]]

Freud, Psychoanalysis and Death. By Liran Razinsky. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2013, xxii + 303 pp., $114.99 hardcover, $41.99 paperback.

Anton Kris

Anton Kris

16 Channing Place
Cambridge, MA 02138-3307
E-mail: aok@kris.org

Anton Kris   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Professor of Psychiatry (part-time), Harvard Medical School.

This is an ambitious book. “It aims to show how and why death has been marginalized or repressed in psychoanalysis” (p. 1). The author, having worked on his subject for over a decade, is enormously well informed and thoughtful. Although he vigorously challenges Freud's view of death in

447

psychic life, he does so with respect and, especially toward the end of the book, with increasing admiration.

He finds that Freud fails to attend to death:

It all starts from a few statements by Freud about death's inaccessibility to the unconscious (1915, pp. 289, 296-7; 1923, pp. 57-9; 1926, pp. 129-30, 140). Death is negative, abstract, it involves time, and cannot, therefore, be part of unconscious thought. Accordingly, if people are afraid of death, these fears should be understood as secondary, as indications of another “deeper” problem, mostly castration. This is where it all starts but not where it all ends. For this position reverberates throughout the history of psychoanalysis, finding various expressions. It consists in a kind of disbelief in death, an unwillingness to recognize death as a possibly influential psychic factor [pp. 1-2].

Then, again, he writes:

However, as sometimes happens, Freud's position on death is more ambivalent, vacillating, and complex than the positions of many of his followers. Even when reductionistic, it is multifariously so. Freud's work, particularly in its less reductionistic manifestations, contains fruitful intuitions and sharp insights into the nature of our attitude toward death. It often betrays a tension between reductionistic tendencies and attempts to attribute the utmost significance to death. Many times, only Freud's more reductionistic statements were adopted by future generations of theoreticians and analysts [p. 2].

This is a thoughtful, scholarly work but, perhaps because it is a compilation of many individual publications, unnecessarily repetitive. The author tends to present so many sides to his argument, crediting with one hand, dismissing with the other, that it is frequently hard to accept his conclusions.

[[RunningHead]]

The Relevance of Bionian Thinking to the Treatment of PTSD Patients

David Potik

David Potik

Weizzman 6
Tel Aviv 64239
ISRAEL
E-mail: davidpo@tlc.gov.il

David Potik  

PTSD, catastrophic change, dissociation, alpha function, narrative exposure therapy, K

Analysts who conduct trauma-focused treatments have much to learn from Wilfred Bion's contributions to the understanding and treatment of PTSD. Concepts such as catastrophe and catastrophic change can illuminate the subjective experience of PTSD patients, the beta screen concept sheds light on dissociative phenomena, and, as shown in a clinical vignette, the analyst can use his alpha function to transform the patient's fragmented raw material into alpha elements. A second clinical vignette shows how during a cognitive intervention the analyst can impart this alpha function to the patient and use his reverie to help the patient assimilate previously intolerable fragments into a coherent narrative. Finally, the idea of knowing mental pain (Bion's K) is discussed in relation to the processing of traumatic memories.

[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Attitude, Theory, and Technique

A Psychoanalyst in the Classroom: On the Human Condition in Education. By Deborah P. Britzman. Albany: SUNY Press, 2015, x + 178 pp., $49.99 hardcover, $23.95 paperback.

Jeffrey Berman and Paul Mosher

Paul Mosher

Albany, NY 12222
E-mail: jberman@albany.edu

6 Woodlawn Avenue
Albany, NY 12208
E-mail: paulw@mosher.com

Paul Mosher   Private practice, Albany, NY; former Chair, Committee on Cofidentiality, American Psychoanalytic Association.

In his late essay “Analysis Terminable and Interminable” (1937), Freud famously referred to psychoanalysis as the third of the “impossible” professions; the other two, he adds, “which have been known much longer,” are education and governance. Freud asks why they are impossible professions and then answers his own question: “one can be sure beforehand of achieving unsatisfying results” (p. 248). Deborah Britzman has expertise in two of these impossible professions. She is Distinguished Research Professor of Education at York University, Toronto, and a psychoanalyst. To add to her credentials, she is a Fellow of the Royal Society of Canada. For years Britzman has been writing intellectually and emotionally challenging books about how to transform impossible classrooms into sites of creative learning (see, e.g., Britzman 1998, 2009). Her latest book, A Psychoanalyst in the Classroom: On the Human Condition in Education, continues her rigorous explorations, showing how psychoanalysis and pedagogy enable us to understand an “unconscious education.”

As Britzman notes in chapter 1, her book focuses on both the university classroom and clinical practice. She recognizes that while clinical and pedagogical work are not identical, both may be “taken personally,” by which she means that both reveal subjectivity and intersubjectivity. She quotes Christopher Bollas's observation in Forces of Destiny (1991): “It is next to impossible to account for what transpires in a psychoanalysis” (p. 7; quoted on p. 38). As every teacher knows, the same observation may be made about education: it is next to impossible to account for what transpires in a classroom.

[[RunningHead]]

Grief and Its Transcendence: Memory, Identity, Creativity. Edited by Adele Tutter and Léon Wurmser. New York: Routledge, 2016, xliv + 212 pp., $.190.00 hardcover, $52.95 paperback.

Murray Schwartz

Murray Schwartz

108 Lessey Street
Amherst, MA 01002
E-mail: murray.schwartz@gmail.com

Murray Schwartz   Professor emeritus, Emerson College; Scholar Member and Coordinator, Center for Multi-disciplinary Psychoanalytic Studies (COMPASS), Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute.

In the second scene of Shakespeare's Hamlet, Claudius, seeming in masterful control of courtly business, glibly reminds Hamlet in his “inky cloak” of the universality of death and mourning, “since the first cor[p]se” (1.2.105). Loss of fathers is common; grieving for a limited time prescribed; life moves on. “Why seems it so particular to thee?” (1.2.75), says Gertrude to her son in this play of “maimed rites” (5.1.12). Yet Hamlet is unconsoled. He has “that within which passes show” (1.2.85). In the world of this tragedy, both the intense particularity of grief and the human need for shared mourning are poisoned by guilt and the hasty appearance of renewal. How can such tragic failures be transcended? How can grief find, in its own time, adequate symbolic representation and communal recognition?

Grief and Its Transcendence: Memory, Identity, Creativity, a superb collection of essays edited by Adele Tutter and Léon Wurmser, explores these questions with theoretical sophistication, historical range, and interdisciplinary richness. In an expansive development of a panel held at the Winter Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association in January 2013 (see Nimroody 2014), Tutter and Wurmser have assembled writings (and over three dozen black-and-white illustrations) that cover mythology, literature, poetry, history, anthropology, theology, photography, music, painting, and sculpture, as well as clinical experiences and theoretical reflections from several perspectives. Most important, within its broad compass, the “personal specificity” (p. xxxiii) of loss, trauma, and creative responses pervades the volume. Though Freud's “Mourning and Melancholia” (1917) remains a touchstone, the essays are intended to move well beyond Freud's formula of successful mourning as an acceptance of finality of loss (“the verdict of reality that the object no longer exists” [p. 255], a view his own painful experience proved incomplete) to explore individual and collective ways in which the suffering of absence can be transformed into symbolic presence and renewed relationships in one's inner world and between self and others.

[[RunningHead]]

Training Analysts at William Alanson White

Nancy McWilliams

Nancy McWilliams

9 Mine Street
Flemington, NJ 08822-1515
E-mail: nancymcw@aol.com

Nancy McWilliams  

In my commentary on Otto Kernberg and Robert Michels in JAPA 64/3, I unwittingly misrepresented the educational policies of the William Alanson White Institute.

Ruth Imber, chair of the Appointments and Promotions Committee at William Alanson White, has clarified that the institute does in fact have a long-standing internal training analyst (TA) system, comparable to that of other institutes within the American Psychoanalytic Association. The sole exception involves the rule requiring external certification for APsaA institute graduates seeking TA status. When White joined APsaA in January 2015, all current TAs were grandfathered in, bypassing the APsA certification requirement. This arrangement will be in force for all White graduates who go through the internal screening TA process for the ten years following that date.

Nancy McWilliams

9 Mine Street

Flemington, NJ 08822-1515
